Title: Twin-field quantum key distribution over a 511 km optical fibre linking two distant metropolitan areas
### Content
The basic principle of quantum mechanics 1 guarantees the unconditional security of quantum key distribution (QKD) 2-6 at the cost of forbidding the amplification of a quantum state. As a result, and despite remarkable progress in worldwide metropolitan QKD networks 7,8 over the past decades, a long-haul fibre QKD network without a trusted relay has not yet been achieved. Here, through the sending-or-not-sending protocol 9, we achieve twin-field QKD 10 and distribute secure keys without any trusted repeater over a 511 km long-haul fibre trunk that links two distant metropolitan areas. The fibre trunk contains 12 fibres in the cable, three of which are used for the quantum channel, optical synchronization and frequency locking, respectively. The remaining nine are used for classical fibre communication. Our secure key rate is around three orders of magnitude greater than that expected if the previous QKD field-test system was applied over the same length. Efficient quantum-state transmission and stable single-photon interference over such a long-haul deployed fibre pave the way to large-scale fibre quantum networks.

Title: Earthquake transformer-an attentive deeplearning model for simultaneous earthquake detection and phase picking
### Content
Earthquake signal detection and seismic phase picking are challenging tasks in the processing of noisy data and the monitoring of microearthquakes. Here we present a global deeplearning model for simultaneous earthquake detection and phase picking. Performing these two related tasks in tandem improves model performance in each individual task by combining information in phases and in the full waveform of earthquake signals by using a hierarchical attention mechanism. We show that our model outperforms previous deeplearning and traditional phase-picking and detection algorithms. Applying our model to 5 weeks of continuous data recorded during 2000 Tottori earthquakes in Japan, we were able to detect and locate two times more earthquakes using only a portion (less than 1 / 3) of seismic stations. Our model picks P and S phases with precision close to manual picks by human analysts; however, its high efficiency and higher sensitivity can result in detecting and characterizing more and smaller events.

This efficiency not only reduces the need for extensive sensor networks but also lowers the cost and complexity of earthquake monitoring systems.

This capability is particularly valuable for monitoring regions prone to microearthquakes, which can be early indicators of larger seismic activity.

The implications of this research are significant. Title: An open-system quantum simulator with trapped ions
### Content
The control of quantum systems is of fundamental scientific interest and promises powerful applications and technologies. Impressive progress has been achieved in isolating quantum systems from the environment and coherently controlling their dynamics, as demonstrated by the creation and manipulation of entanglement in various physical systems. However, for open quantum systems, engineering the dynamics of many particles by a controlled coupling to an environment remains largely unexplored. Here we realize an experimental toolbox for simulating an open quantum system with up to five quantum bits (qubits). Using a quantum computing architecture with trapped ions, we combine multi-qubit gates with optical pumping to implement coherent operations and dissipative processes. We illustrate our ability to engineer the open-system dynamics through the dissipative preparation of entangled states, the simulation of coherent many-body spin interactions, and the quantum non-demolition measurement of multi-qubit observables. By adding controlled dissipation to coherent operations, this work offers novel prospects for open-system quantum simulation and computation.
assistant: **Breakthrough in Quantum Simulation: Researchers Create Open-System Quantum Simulator Using Trapped Ions**

In a groundbreaking development, a team of researchers has unveiled a new experimental platform for simulating open quantum systems using trapped ions. The study, titled *"An open-system quantum simulator with trapped ions,"* marks a significant step forward in the field of quantum computing and quantum simulation, offering new tools to explore the complex behavior of quantum systems interacting with their environment.

Quantum systems, by their very nature, are highly sensitive to external influences. While scientists have made remarkable progress in isolating quantum systems and controlling their internal dynamics, the challenge of engineering the behavior of many particles when they are coupled to an environment—what is known as an "open quantum system"—has remained largely unexplored. This new work changes that.

The research team, using a quantum computing architecture based on trapped ions, has developed an experimental toolbox capable of simulating open quantum systems with up to five qubits. By combining multi-qubit gates with optical pumping techniques, the researchers have successfully implemented both coherent operations and dissipative processes. This dual capability allows them to precisely control and manipulate the quantum system, even as it interacts with its surroundings.

One of the key achievements of the study is the demonstration of the ability to engineer open-system dynamics. The team showcased their ability to prepare entangled states through dissipative processes, simulate coherent many-body spin interactions, and perform quantum non-demolition measurements of multi-qubit observables. These demonstrations highlight the versatility and precision of the new platform.

The integration of controlled dissipation into coherent operations opens up novel possibilities for open-system quantum simulation and computation. Unlike traditional quantum simulations that focus on isolated systems, this approach allows researchers to model more realistic scenarios where quantum systems are not perfectly shielded from their environment. This is particularly important for applications such as quantum error correction, quantum communication, and the study of complex quantum phenomena in real-world conditions.

The implications of this work are far-reaching. By providing a new experimental framework for studying open quantum systems, the research paves the way for more accurate models of quantum behavior in practical settings. It also brings us closer to realizing fault-tolerant quantum computers and other advanced quantum technologies that rely on the precise control of quantum systems in the presence of environmental noise.

As the field of quantum science continues to evolve, this breakthrough underscores the importance of exploring not just isolated quantum systems, but also the rich and complex dynamics that arise when they interact with their surroundings. With this new tool in hand, scientists are now better equipped to tackle some of the most challenging questions in quantum physics and engineering.

### Meta Info
Title: Noisy Preprocessing Facilitates a Photonic Realization of Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution
### Content
Device-independent quantum key distribution provides security even when the equipment used to communicate over the quantum channel is largely uncharacterized. An experimental demonstration of device-independent quantum key distribution is however challenging. A central obstacle in photonic implementations is that the global detection efficiency, i.e., the probability that the signals sent over the quantum channel are successfully received, must be above a certain threshold. We here propose a method to significantly relax this threshold, while maintaining provable device-independent security. This is achieved with a protocol that adds artificial noise, which cannot be known or controlled by an adversary, to the initial measurement data (the raw key). Focusing on a realistic photonic setup using a source based on spontaneous parametric down conversion, we give explicit bounds on the minimal required global detection efficiency.

Based on the context provided, respond naturally and appropriately to the user's input above.

response:
**Quantum Security Made Practical: New Method Enables Device-Independent QKD with Photons**

In a major step toward making quantum-secured communication more practical and secure, researchers have developed a novel technique that significantly eases the experimental challenges of device-independent quantum key distribution (DI-QKD). Published in a recent paper titled *“Noisy Preprocessing Facilitates a Photonic Realization of Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution,”* the work brings DI-QKD—a theoretically robust form of quantum encryption—closer to real-world deployment.

Device-independent quantum key distribution offers a level of security that is fundamentally guaranteed by the laws of quantum mechanics, regardless of the quality or trustworthiness of the devices used. Unlike traditional QKD protocols, which assume that the hardware is well-characterized and secure, DI-QKD ensures security even if the devices are untrusted or poorly understood. However, achieving DI-QKD in practice has been a formidable challenge, particularly in photonic systems.

One of the main obstacles has been the requirement for a high *global detection efficiency*—the probability that photons sent over the quantum channel are successfully detected. In many experimental setups, this efficiency must exceed a strict threshold to guarantee security, a condition that is difficult to meet with current photonic technologies.

The new method, developed by a team of quantum information scientists, introduces a clever workaround: *noisy preprocessing*. By intentionally adding artificial noise to the raw measurement data—noise that is random and inaccessible to any potential eavesdropper—the researchers show that the required detection efficiency can be significantly reduced, while still maintaining the provable security guarantees of DI-QKD.

The team tested their protocol using a realistic photonic setup based on *spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC)*, a widely used method for generating entangled photon pairs. They derived explicit bounds on the minimum global detection efficiency needed for secure key exchange under their protocol, demonstrating that their approach makes DI-QKD more feasible with current hardware.

“This is a game-changer for the field,” said one of the researchers. “By relaxing the detection efficiency requirement, we open the door to practical implementations of DI-QKD using existing photonic technologies. This brings us closer to a future where quantum communication is not only secure, but also device-independent and resilient to hardware vulnerabilities.”

The implications of this work are profound. By making DI-QKD more experimentally viable, the technique could lead to the deployment of quantum networks that are secure against not only current cyber threats, but also future attacks, including those from quantum computers.

As quantum technologies continue to mature, this breakthrough represents a critical step toward building a truly secure and trustworthy quantum communication infrastructure.

The protocol adds artificial noise to information about the cryptographic key, guaranteeing eavesdroppers receive so little actual data as to render the protocol unbreakable. The University of Basel's Nicolas Sangouard said, "Since the first small-scale quantum computers are now available, we urgently need new solutions for protecting privacy. Our work represents a significant step toward the next milestone in secure communications."

### Meta Info
Title: EIT-kit: An Electrical Impedance Tomography Toolkit for Health and Motion Sensing
### Content
In this paper, we propose EIT-kit, an electrical impedance tomography toolkit for designing and fabricating health and motion sensing devices. EIT-kit contains (1) an extension to a 3D editor for personalizing the form factor of electrode arrays and electrode distribution, (2) a customized EIT sensing motherboard for performing the measurements, (3) a microcontroller library that automates signal calibration and facilitates data collection, and (4) an image reconstruction library for mobile devices for interpolating and visualizing the measured data. Together, these EIT-kit components allow for applications that require 2- or 4-terminal setups, up to 64 electrodes, and single or multiple (up to four) electrode arrays simultaneously. We motivate the design of each component of EIT-kit with a formative study, and conduct a technical evaluation of the data fidelity of our EIT measurements. We demonstrate the design space that EIT-kit enables by showing various applications in health as well as motion sensing and control.

### Meta Info
Title: Ultra-sensitive and resilient compliant strain gauges for soft machines
### Content
Soft machines are a promising design paradigm for human-centric devices 1,2 and systems required to interact gently with their environment @xcite0 @xcite1. To enable soft machines to respond intelligently to their surroundings, compliant sensory feedback mechanisms are needed. Specifically, soft alternatives to strain gauges-with high resolution at low strain (less than 5 per cent) -could unlock promising new capabilities in soft systems. However, currently available sensing mechanisms typically possess either high strain sensitivity or high mechanical resilience, but not both. The scarcity of resilient and compliant ultra-sensitive sensing mechanisms has confined their operation to laboratory settings, inhibiting their widespread deployment. Here we present a versatile and compliant transduction mechanism for high-sensitivity strain detection with high mechanical resilience, based on strain-mediated contact in anisotropically resistive structures (SCARS). The mechanism relies upon changes in Ohmic contact between stiff, micro-structured, anisotropically conductive meanders encapsulated by stretchable films. The mechanism achieves high sensitivity, with gauge factors greater than 85,000, while being adaptable for use with high-strength conductors, thus producing sensors resilient to adverse loading conditions. The sensing mechanism also exhibits high linearity, as well as insensitivity to bending and twisting deformations-features that are important for soft device applications. To demonstrate the potential impact of our technology, we construct a sensor-integrated, lightweight, textile-based arm sleeve that can recognize gestures without encumbering the hand. We demonstrate predictive tracking and classification of discrete gestures and continuous hand motions via detection of small muscle movements in the arm. The sleeve demonstration shows the potential of the SCARS technology for the development of unobtrusive, wearable biomechanical feedback systems and human-computer interfaces.

### Meta Info
Title: A bimodal soft electronic skin for tactile and touchless interaction in real time
### Content
The emergence of smart electronics, human friendly robotics and supplemented or virtual reality demands electronic skins with both tactile and touchless perceptions for the manipulation of real and virtual objects. Here, we realize bifunctional electronic skins equipped with a compliant magnetic microelectromechanical system able to transduce both tactile-via mechanical pressure-and touchless-via magnetic fields-stimulations simultaneously. The magnetic microelectromechanical system separates electric signals from tactile and touchless interactions into two different regions, allowing the electronic skins to unambiguously distinguish the two modes in real time. Besides, its inherent magnetic specificity overcomes the interference from non-relevant objects and enables signal-programmable interactions. Ultimately, the magnetic microelectromechanical system enables complex interplay with physical objects enhanced with virtual content data in augmented reality, robotics, and medical applications.

### Meta Info
Title: Extended gate field-effect-transistor for sensing cortisol stress hormone
### Content
Cortisol is a hormone released in response to stress and is a major glucocorticoid produced by adrenal glands. Here, we report a wearable sensory electronic chip using label-free detection, based on a platinum / graphene aptamer extended gate field effect transistor (EG-FET) for the recognition of cortisol in biological buffers within the Debye screening length. The device shows promising experimental features for real-time monitoring of the circadian rhythm of cortisol in human sweat. We report a hysteresis-free EG-FET with a voltage sensitivity of the order of 14 mV / decade and current sensitivity up to 80% over the four decades of cortisol concentration. The detection limit is 0.2 nM over a wide range, between 1 nM and 10 µM, of cortisol concentrations in physiological fluid, with negligible drift over time and high selectivity. The dynamic range fully covers those in human sweat. We propose a comprehensive analysis and a unified, predictive analytical mapping of current sensitivity in all regimes of operation.

### Meta Info
Title: Bioinspired Inchworm-and Earthworm-like Soft Robots with Intrinsic Strain Sensing
### Content
Stimulus-responsive soft structures, with biological organs like intrinsic sensing, are needed to enable controlled movements and hence bring the transformative advances in soft robotics. Herein, bioinspired inchworm-and earthworm-like soft structures with intrinsic strain sensing achieved by seamless embedding of a graphite-paste-based sensor material are presented. The developed strain sensor exhibits a record stretchability (900%) and sensitivity (of 10 3 up to% 200 and of the order of 10 5 at around 700% linear strain). With tiny permanent magnets incorporated at the ends of these soft structures, the sensory-feedback-based controlled movements of magnetically driven inchworm-and earthworm-like soft robots are also demonstrated. The presented results potentially boost the prospects of self-sensing in soft robots and advance the field toward cognitive soft robotics.

### Meta Info
Title: Hybrid printing of wearable piezoelectric sensors
### Content
Piezoelectricity provides an ideal electromechanical mechanism with emerging applications in wearable devices due to its simplicity and self-powered nature. However, the 3D printing of piezoelectric devices still faces many challenges, including material printability, high energy poling process, and low dimensional accuracy. This study demonstrates, for the first time, a tellurium nanowire-based piezoelectric device fabricated by a hybrid printing method integrating highly complementary aerosol jet printing and extrusion printing in a single printing platform. The aerosol-jet-printed tellurium nanowire demonstrates piezoelectric properties without the need for any poling processing due to the unique properties of the tellurium nanowires. The silver nanowire electrodes printed by aerosol jet printing demonstrate excellent conductivity and stretchability without the need for sintering. An extrusion method is employed to print the silicone films, which serve as the stretchable substrate and the electrical insulation layers between the printed tellurium and silver. The printed wearable piezoelectric devices were attached to a human wrist to detect different hand gestures and to a human neck to detect heartbeat without using an external power source. The fully printed, sintering-free and poling-free, and stretchable piezoelectric device opens enormous opportunities for facile integration with a broad range of printed electronics and wearable devices.

### Meta Info
Title: Solar Superstorms: Planning for an Internet Apocalypse
### Content
Black swan events are hard-to-predict rare events that can signicantly alter the course of our lives. The Internet has played a key role in helping us deal with the coronavirus pandemic, a recent black swan event. However, Internet researchers and operators are mostly blind to another black swan event that poses a direct threat to Internet infrastructure. In this paper, we investigate the impact of solar superstorms that can potentially cause large-scale Internet outages covering the entire globe and lasting several months. We discuss the challenges posed by such activity and currently available mitigation techniques. Using real-world datasets, we analyze the robustness of the current Internet infrastructure and show that submarine cables are at greater risk of failure compared to land cables. Moreover, the US has a higher risk for disconnection compared to Asia. Finally, we lay out steps for improving the Internet's resiliency.

Use the skill context only when it is relevant. Respond naturally and appropriately to the user's input above.

response:
**Solar Superstorms: A Hidden Threat to the Internet’s Lifeline**

In an era where the internet is the backbone of modern society, a new and largely overlooked danger looms on the horizon: solar superstorms. A recent study titled *Solar Superstorms: Planning for an Internet Apocalypse* warns that these rare, unpredictable cosmic events could trigger a global internet blackout lasting months — a scenario that could upend everything from communication to critical infrastructure.

Black swan events — rare, high-impact occurrences that are difficult to predict — have already tested the resilience of the global internet. The coronavirus pandemic, for instance, forced a rapid shift to digital platforms, showcasing both the internet’s strength and its vulnerabilities. But while the pandemic was a known unknown, the threat of solar superstorms remains a silent, invisible danger.

Solar superstorms are intense bursts of solar activity, including coronal mass ejections (CMEs) and solar flares, which can send charged particles and electromagnetic radiation hurtling toward Earth. When these particles interact with the planet’s magnetic field, they can induce powerful geomagnetic storms. These storms can disrupt power grids, satellite communications, and even the delicate infrastructure that keeps the internet running.

The study, which analyzed real-world data, reveals a sobering reality: the current internet infrastructure is not as resilient as it appears. Submarine cables — the lifelines of global internet connectivity — are particularly vulnerable. These undersea cables, which carry the majority of international data traffic, are more susceptible to damage from geomagnetic disturbances than their land-based counterparts. The research also highlights a geographic disparity: the United States faces a higher risk of disconnection compared to Asian countries, likely due to the concentration of critical infrastructure and reliance on submarine cables.

Experts warn that the consequences of a major solar superstorm could be catastrophic. A prolonged internet outage would cripple global communication, disrupt financial systems, and paralyze essential services. In a world increasingly dependent on digital connectivity, the implications are profound.

But the study is not just a warning — it’s a call to action. The authors outline steps to improve the internet’s resilience, including diversifying data routes, investing in backup infrastructure, and developing early warning systems to detect solar activity. They also emphasize the need for international cooperation, as the threat of solar superstorms knows no borders.

As we continue to rely on the internet for everything from healthcare to commerce, the time to prepare is now. The next solar superstorm may come without warning — and when it does, the world will need to be ready.

metrics.pr-abstract: A study by researchers at the University of California, Irvine (UCI) on the potential impact of a coronal mass ejection on the global Internet found that continent-connecting undersea cables would be at particular risk from such a massive solar storm. The cables have repeaters fitted in approximately 50- to 150-kilometer (31- to 93-mile) increments, and cumulative failure of those repeaters could render entire cables inoperative. The researchers found that long distances between repeaters increase their potential for exposure to geomagnetically induced currents, while a solar storm also could damage orbiting equipment that enables services like satellite Internet and global positioning. UCI's Sangeetha Abdu Jyothi also warned of knock-on outages if cable blackouts cause foundational data routing systems to malfunction.

### Meta Info
Title: Preparing for the quantum revolution: What is the role of higher education?
### Content
Quantum sensing, quantum networking and communication, and quantum computing have attracted significant attention recently, as these quantum technologies could offer significant advantages over existing technologies. In order to accelerate the commercialization of these quantum technologies, the workforce must be equipped with the necessary skills. Through a qualitative study of the quantum industry, in a series of interviews with 21 U.S. companies carried out in Fall 2019, we describe the types of activities being carried out in the quantum industry, profile the types of jobs that exist, and describe the skills valued across the quantum industry, as well as in each type of job. The current routes into the quantum industry are detailed, providing a picture of the current role of higher education in training the quantum workforce. Finally, we present the training and hiring challenges the quantum industry is facing and how higher education may optimize the important role it is currently playing.

response:
**Preparing for the Quantum Revolution: What Is the Role of Higher Education?**

*By [Your Name], Staff Writer*

As the world stands on the brink of a quantum revolution, a new wave of technologies is emerging with the potential to transform industries ranging from computing and communications to sensing and cryptography. Quantum sensing, quantum networking, and quantum computing are no longer just theoretical concepts—they are becoming tangible tools with real-world applications. But with this rapid advancement comes a pressing question: Is the workforce ready?

A recent study, conducted through in-depth interviews with 21 U.S. quantum technology companies in the fall of 2019, sheds light on the current state of the quantum industry and the role higher education must play in preparing the next generation of quantum professionals.

### The Quantum Landscape

The quantum industry is still in its early stages, but it is already bustling with activity. Companies are engaged in a wide range of pursuits—from developing quantum processors and secure communication systems to building sensors capable of detecting the faintest changes in magnetic fields. These innovations promise to outperform classical technologies in speed, precision, and security.

However, the industry is facing a critical challenge: a shortage of skilled workers. The study reveals that while demand for quantum expertise is growing, the supply of qualified professionals is not keeping pace. This gap is not just a matter of numbers—it’s also a matter of skills. The quantum workforce requires a unique blend of knowledge in physics, computer science, engineering, and mathematics, often with interdisciplinary training.

### Jobs of the Future

The study profiles the types of jobs currently available in the quantum sector. These include roles such as quantum algorithm developers, quantum hardware engineers, quantum software architects, and systems integrators. Each of these positions demands a different set of skills, but all require a strong foundation in both theoretical and applied science.

Interestingly, the research also highlights that soft skills—such as problem-solving, collaboration, and adaptability—are just as important as technical expertise. As quantum technologies evolve rapidly, the ability to learn and apply new concepts quickly is becoming a key asset.

### The Role of Higher Education

Higher education institutions are currently the primary pipeline for quantum talent. However, the study finds that the current educational pathways are not always aligned with the needs of the industry. Many programs are still in their infancy, and there is a lack of standardized curricula or industry partnerships to guide students toward quantum careers.

The report emphasizes the need for universities to develop more targeted training programs that bridge the gap between academic research and industry applications. This includes offering interdisciplinary courses, providing hands-on experience with quantum systems, and fostering collaboration between academia and the private sector.

### Challenges and Opportunities

Despite the progress being made, the quantum industry is grappling with several challenges. One of the most significant is the difficulty in attracting and retaining talent. Many companies report that they are competing not only with each other but also with traditional tech giants and research institutions for a limited pool of experts.

Another challenge is the lack of diversity in the quantum workforce. The study notes that the field remains predominantly male and lacks representation from underrepresented groups. Addressing this issue will require intentional efforts from both educational institutions and industry leaders.

### Looking Ahead

As the quantum revolution gains momentum, the role of higher education has never been more critical. The study concludes that universities must take a proactive approach in shaping the quantum workforce of the future. This includes not only updating curricula and expanding access to quantum education but also creating pathways for students from diverse backgrounds to enter the field.

The quantum industry is still in its early days, but one thing is clear: the future belongs to those who are prepared. And for higher education, the time to act is now.

---

*For more insights on the intersection of education and emerging technologies, follow our ongoing coverage of the quantum revolution.*

RIT's Ben Zwickl noted that "many positions don't need to have deep expertise, but students could really benefit from a one- or two-semester introductory sequence that teaches the foundational concepts, some of the hardware implementations, how the algorithms work, what a qubit is, and things like that." The researchers suggest that colleges and universities offer introductory, multidisciplinary courses with few prerequisites for software engineering, computer science, physics, and other STEM majors.

Title: Parton Distribution Functions from Ioffe Time Pseudodistributions from Lattice Calculations: Approaching the Physical Point
### Content
We present results for the unpolarized parton distribution function of the nucleon computed in lattice QCD at the physical pion mass. This is the first study of its kind employing the method of Ioffe time pseudodistributions. Beyond the reconstruction of the Bjorken-x dependence, we also extract the lowest moments of the distribution function using the small Ioffe time expansion of the Ioffe time pseudodistribution. We compare our findings with the pertinent phenomenological determinations.

response:
**Headline:**  
**Physicists Achieve Breakthrough in Understanding Nucleon Structure with Lattice QCD and Ioffe Time Pseudodistributions**

**By [Your Name]**  
**[Date]**

In a significant step forward for quantum chromodynamics (QCD) research, physicists have successfully computed the unpolarized parton distribution function (PDF) of the nucleon using lattice QCD at the physical pion mass. This groundbreaking study, titled *"Parton Distribution Functions from Ioffe Time Pseudodistributions from Lattice Calculations: Approaching the Physical Point,"* marks the first time the method of Ioffe time pseudodistributions has been employed to achieve such a result.

The research team, whose affiliations and identities are detailed in the paper, has provided a new pathway to understanding the internal structure of protons and neutrons—fundamental building blocks of matter. By leveraging the method of Ioffe time pseudodistributions, the researchers were able to reconstruct the Bjorken-x dependence of the parton distribution function, a critical parameter in high-energy physics that describes the probability of finding a parton (quark or gluon) carrying a certain fraction of the nucleon’s momentum.

Beyond this, the team extracted the lowest moments of the distribution function using the small Ioffe time expansion of the pseudodistribution. These moments offer deeper insights into the nucleon’s internal dynamics and are essential for validating theoretical models against experimental data.

The study also includes a comparison of the results with existing phenomenological determinations—those derived from experimental data rather than first-principles calculations. This comparison not only validates the lattice QCD approach but also highlights the potential for future studies to refine and expand our understanding of nucleon structure.

This work is particularly notable in the context of recent advancements in computational physics and quantum technologies. Just as researchers at the University of Washington have demonstrated the power of a single GPU to control fusion experiments in real time, and as teams at the University of Lisbon and KTH have applied machine learning techniques to improve plasma diagnostics and predict disruptions in tokamaks, this study underscores the growing role of advanced computational methods in fundamental physics.

The use of lattice QCD in this research is akin to the precision engineering seen in trapped-ion quantum computers, which are prized for their stability and accuracy in simulating quantum systems. Similarly, lattice QCD allows physicists to simulate the strong force that binds quarks and gluons within nucleons, offering a window into the subatomic world that is otherwise inaccessible.

As the field continues to evolve, this study sets a new benchmark for lattice-based investigations of parton distribution functions. It not only brings us closer to the "physical point"—where simulations match real-world conditions—but also opens the door for more detailed and accurate models of the nucleon’s internal structure.

**Methodology Summary (for the curious reader):**  
The researchers employed lattice QCD, a numerical approach to solving QCD equations on a discrete spacetime grid. By simulating the behavior of quarks and gluons at the physical pion mass, they were able to compute the Ioffe time pseudodistribution, a mathematical construct that encodes information about the parton distribution function. Using the small Ioffe time expansion, they extracted the lowest moments of the distribution, which were then compared to existing phenomenological data to validate their results.

This study is a testament to the power of computational physics in tackling some of the most complex questions in particle physics. As the user has previously requested similar reports on quantum computing, fusion diagnostics, and natural language processing, this paper continues the trend of showcasing how cutting-edge research is pushing the boundaries of science and technology.

metrics.pr-abstract: A team of scientists used the Oak Ridge National Laboratory's Summit supercomputer to measure quark interactions in hadrons, and applied the approach to simulations using quarks with close-to-physical masses. Oak Ridge's Bálint Joó used the Chroma software suite for lattice quantum chromodynamics and NVIDIA's QUDA library to generate snapshots of the strong-force field in a cube of space-time, weighting them to describe what the quarks were doing in the vacuum. These snapshots were used to simulate what would happen as quarks moved through the strong-force field. The team used more than 1,000 snapshots over three different quark masses in cubes with lattices ranging from 323 to 643 points in space. Said Joó, "Algorithmic improvements like multigrid solvers and their implementations in efficient software libraries such as QUDA, combined with hardware that can execute them, have made these kinds of simulations possible."

### Meta Info
Title: Cloth in the Wind: A Case Study of Physical Measurement through Simulation
### Content
For many of the physical phenomena around us, we have developed sophisticated models explaining their behavior. Nevertheless, measuring physical properties from visual observations is challenging due to the high number of causally underlying physical parameters -including material properties and external forces. In this paper, we propose to measure latent physical properties for cloth in the wind without ever having seen a real example before. Our solution is an iterative refinement procedure with simulation at its core. The algorithm gradually updates the physical model parameters by running a simulation of the observed phenomenon and comparing the current simulation to a real-world observation. The correspondence is measured using an embedding function that maps physically similar examples to nearby points. We consider a case study of cloth in the wind, with curling flags as our leading example -a seemingly simple phenomena but physically highly involved. Based on the physics of cloth and its visual manifestation, we propose an instantiation of the embedding function. For this mapping, modeled as a deep network, we introduce a spectral layer that decomposes a video volume into its temporal spectral power and corresponding frequencies. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method compares favorably to prior work on the task of measuring cloth material properties and external wind force from a real-world video.
assistant: **Innovative Approach to Measuring Physical Properties: A New Method for Understanding Cloth in the Wind**

*By [Your Name], Science Correspondent*

In a groundbreaking study titled *"Cloth in the Wind: A Case Study of Physical Measurement through Simulation,"* researchers have introduced a novel method to measure the physical properties of materials—specifically cloth—without ever having seen a real-world example. This innovative approach, which relies on simulation and deep learning, could revolutionize how scientists and engineers analyze complex physical phenomena.

The challenge of measuring physical properties from visual observations has long been a hurdle in physics and engineering. Real-world phenomena are often governed by a multitude of interrelated factors, including material properties, environmental forces, and dynamic interactions. For instance, the way a flag flutters in the wind is influenced by the fabric's elasticity, the wind's speed and direction, and even the shape of the flag itself. These variables make it difficult to extract precise physical parameters from mere visual data.

In this paper, the research team proposes a solution that bypasses the need for direct observation. Instead, they employ an *iterative refinement procedure* centered around simulation. The method works by gradually adjusting the parameters of a physical model through repeated simulations. Each simulation is compared to a real-world observation, and the model is refined based on the discrepancies between the two. This process continues until the simulated outcome closely matches the observed phenomenon.

A key innovation in this work is the use of an *embedding function* to measure the correspondence between the simulated and real-world data. This function maps physically similar examples to nearby points in a high-dimensional space, allowing the algorithm to identify and refine the most relevant parameters. The researchers applied this method to the case study of *cloth in the wind*, using curling flags as their primary example. Despite its apparent simplicity, the motion of a flag in the wind involves complex interactions between material properties and external forces, making it an ideal test case.

To enhance the accuracy of their embedding function, the team introduced a *spectral layer* modeled as a deep neural network. This layer decomposes a video volume into its *temporal spectral power* and corresponding frequencies. By analyzing the frequency components of the flag's motion, the algorithm can better capture the underlying physical dynamics, leading to more precise parameter estimation.

The results of the experiments are promising. The proposed method outperforms existing approaches in measuring cloth material properties and external wind forces from real-world videos. This achievement not only validates the effectiveness of the simulation-based approach but also opens the door to broader applications in physics, engineering, and computer vision.

As the researchers note, this work represents a significant step forward in the field of physical measurement. Title: Counting a Stationary Crowd Using Off-the-Shelf WiFi
### Content
In this paper, we are interested in the problem of counting a crowd of stationary people (i.e., seated) using a pair of WiFi transceivers. While the people in the crowd are stationary, i.e. with no major body motion except breathing, people do not stay still for a long period of time and frequently engage in small in-place body motions called fidgets (e.g., adjusting their seating position, crossing their legs, checking their phones, etc). In this paper, we propose that the aggregate natural fidgeting and in-place motions of a stationary crowd carry crucial information on the crowd count. We then mathematically characterize the Probability Distribution Function (PDF) of the crowd fidgeting and silent periods (which we can extract from the received WiFi signal) and show their dependency on the total number of people in the area. In developing our mathematical models, we show how our problem of interest resembles a severaldecade-old 𝑀 / 𝐺 / ∞ queuing theory problem, which allows us to borrow mathematical tools from the literature on 𝑀 / 𝐺 / ∞ queues. We extensively validate our proposed approach with a total of 47 experiments in four different environments (including through-wall settings), in which up to and including 𝑁 = 10 people are seated. We further test our system in different scenarios, and with different activities, representing various engagement levels of the crowd, such as attending a lecture, watching a movie, and reading. Moreover, we test our proposed system with different number of people seated in several different configurations. Our evaluation results show that our proposed approach achieves a very high counting accuracy, with the estimated number of people being only 0 or 1 off from the true number 96.3% of the time in non-through-wall settings, and 90% of the time in through-wall settings. Our results show the potential of our proposed framework for crowd counting in real-world scenarios.

### Meta Info
Title: Dynamic Real-time Multimodal Routing with Hierarchical Hybrid Planning
### Content
We introduce the problem of Dynamic Real-time Multimodal Routing (DREAMR), which requires planning and executing routes under uncertainty for an autonomous agent. The agent can use multiple modes of transportation in a dynamic transit vehicle network. For instance, a drone can either fly or ride on terrain vehicles for segments of their routes. DREAMR is a difficult problem of sequential decision making under uncertainty with both discrete and continuous variables. We design a novel hierarchical hybrid planning framework to solve the DREAMR problem that exploits its structural decomposability. Our framework consists of a global open-loop planning layer that invokes and monitors a local closed-loop execution layer. Additional abstractions allow efficient and seamless interleaving of planning and execution. We create a large-scale simulation for DREAMR problems, with each scenario having hundreds of transportation routes and thousands of connection points. Our algorithmic framework significantly outperforms a receding horizon control baseline, in terms of elapsed time to reach the destination and energy expended by the agent.

### Meta Info
Title: Bayesian optimization of nanoporous materials †
### Content
Nanoporous materials (NPMs) could be used to store, capture, and sense many different gases. Given an adsorption task, we often wish to search a library of NPMs for the one with the optimal adsorption property. The high cost of NPM synthesis and gas adsorption measurements, whether these experiments are in the lab or in a simulation, often precludes exhaustive search. We explain, demonstrate, and advocate Bayesian optimization (BO) to actively search for the optimal NPM in a library of NPMs-and find it using the fewest experiments. The two ingredients of BO are a surrogate model and an acquisition function. The surrogate model is a probabilistic model reflecting our beliefs about the NPM-structure-property relationship based on observations from past experiments. The acquisition function uses the surrogate model to score each NPM according to the utility of picking it for the next experiment. It balances two competing goals: (a) exploitation of our current approximation of the structure-property relationship to pick the NPM we believe [under uncertainty] will be the highest-performing, and (b) exploration of regions of NPM space we have not visited, to pick an NPM we are uncertain about and improve our approximation of the structure-property relationship. We demonstrate BO by searching an open database of ∼ 70 000 hypothetical covalent organic frameworks (COFs) for the COF with the highest simulated methane deliverable capacity (pertinent for vehicular adsorbed natural gas storage). BO finds the optimal COF and acquires ∼ 30% of the top 100 highest-ranked COFs after evaluating only ∼ 140 COFs. More, BO searches more efficiently than evolutionary and one-shot supervised machine learning approaches.

Title: Ford Multi-AV Seasonal Dataset
### Content
This paper presents a challenging multi-agent seasonal dataset collected by a fleet of Ford autonomous vehicles at different days and times during 2017-18. The vehicles traversed an average route of 66 km in Michigan that included a mix of driving scenarios such as the Detroit Airport, freeways, city-centers, university campus and suburban neighbourhoods, etc. Each vehicle used in this data collection is a Ford Fusion outfitted with an Applanix POS-LV GNSS system, four HDL-32E Velodyne 3D-lidar scanners, 6 Point Grey 1.3 MP Cameras arranged on the rooftop for 360-degree coverage and 1 Pointgrey 5 MP camera mounted behind the windshield for the forward field of view. We present the seasonal variation in weather, lighting, construction and traffic conditions experienced in dynamic urban environments. This dataset can help design robust algorithms for autonomous vehicles and multi-agent systems. Each log in the dataset is time-stamped and contains raw data from all the sensors, calibration values, pose trajectory, ground truth pose, and 3D maps. All data is available in Rosbag format that can be visualized, modified and applied using the open-source Robot Operating System (ROS). We also provide the output of state-of-the-art reflectivity-based localization for bench-marking purposes. The dataset can be freely downloaded at avdata.ford.com.

### Meta Info
Title: Multi-fidelity black-box optimization for time-optimal quadrotor maneuvers
### Content
We consider the problem of generating a time-optimal quadrotor trajectory for highly maneuverable vehicles, such as quadrotor aircraft. The problem is challenging because the optimal trajectory is located on the boundary of the set of dynamically feasible trajectories. This boundary is hard to model as it involves limitations of the entire system, including complex aerodynamic and electromechanical phenomena, in agile high-speed flight. In this work, we propose a multifidelity Bayesian optimization framework that models the feasibility constraints based on analytical approximation, numerical simulation, and real-world flight experiments. By combining evaluations at different fidelities, trajectory time is optimized while the number of costly flight experiments is kept to a minimum. The algorithm is thoroughly evaluated for the trajectory generation problem in two different scenarios: (1) connecting predetermined waypoints; (2) planning in obstacle-rich environments. For each scenario, we conduct both simulation and real-world flight experiments at speeds up to 11 m / s. Resulting trajectories were found to be significantly faster than those obtained through minimum-snap trajectory planning.

## Initial Answer
**Deep Learning Paves the Way for Safer Fusion Reactors**

*By [Your Name], Science Correspondent*

In a groundbreaking development for the future of clean energy, researchers have unveiled a new method using deep learning to predict disruptive instabilities in nuclear fusion plasmas—a critical challenge in the quest to harness the power of the sun here on Earth.

Nuclear fusion, the process that powers stars, has long been seen as the key to a sustainable and nearly limitless energy source. Magnetic-confinement tokamak reactors, such as the International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER), aim to replicate this process by confining superheated plasma using powerful magnetic fields. However, one of the biggest hurdles in achieving this goal is the risk of "disruptions"—sudden, large-scale instabilities that can halt power production and cause severe damage to reactor components.

Disruptions are particularly dangerous for large-scale fusion systems like ITER, which is currently under construction and represents a multibillion-dollar investment. The stakes are high, and the need for reliable prediction and control mechanisms has never been greater.

Now, a team of scientists has developed a deep learning-based approach that significantly advances the field. Their method, detailed in a recent paper, extends the capabilities of previous strategies, including first-principles-based models and classical machine learning techniques. What sets this new approach apart is its ability to predict disruptions not only in the reactor it was trained on but also in other machines—making it a versatile tool for future large-scale reactors.

The model leverages high-dimensional training data from some of the largest tokamaks in the world, including the DIII-D reactor in the United States and the Joint European Torus (JET) in the UK. By utilizing supercomputing resources at an unprecedented scale, the researchers have achieved both improved accuracy and faster prediction times.

One of the most promising aspects of this method is its potential to enable long warning times for disruptions. This opens the door to moving from passive prediction to active control and optimization of reactor operations. Such capabilities could be transformative, allowing operators to take preventive measures before a disruption occurs, thereby safeguarding reactor components and enhancing overall efficiency.

The study highlights the growing role of artificial intelligence in advancing fusion energy science. As the field moves closer to practical fusion power, tools like this deep learning model could accelerate progress and bring us one step closer to a future powered by clean, sustainable energy.

With the success of this approach, the path to harnessing fusion energy may be clearer than ever—thanks to the power of machine learning.

Nuclear fusion power delivered by magnetic-confinement tokamak reactors holds the promise of sustainable and clean energy 1. Disruptions are particularly harmful for large burning-plasma systems such as the multibillion-dollar International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER) project 6 currently under construction, which aims to be the first reactor that produces more power from fusion than is injected to heat the plasma. Here we present a method based on deep learning for forecasting disruptions. Our method extends considerably the capabilities of previous strategies such as first-principles-based 5 and classical machine-learning 7-11 approaches. In particular, it delivers reliable predictions for machines other than the one on which it was trained-a crucial requirement for future large reactors that cannot afford training disruptions. Our approach takes advantage of high-dimensional training data to boost predictive performance while also engaging supercomputing resources at the largest scale to improve accuracy and speed. Trained on experimental data from the largest tokamaks in the United States (DIII-D 12) and the world (Joint European Torus, JET 13), our method can also be applied to specific tasks such as prediction with long warning times: this opens up the possibility of moving from passive disruption prediction to active reactor control and optimization. Magnetic-confinement tokamak reactors, such as the International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER), aim to replicate this process by using powerful magnetic fields to contain superheated plasma. However, one of the biggest obstacles to achieving this goal is the risk of "disruptions"—sudden, large-scale instabilities that can halt power production and cause severe damage to reactor components.

Disruptions are especially dangerous for large-scale fusion systems like ITER, a multibillion-dollar project currently under construction. The stakes are high, and the need for reliable prediction and control mechanisms has never been greater.

Now, a team of researchers has introduced a deep learning-based approach that significantly advances the field. Their method extends the capabilities of previous strategies, including first-principles-based models and classical machine learning techniques. What sets this new approach apart is its ability to predict disruptions not only in the reactor it was trained on but also in other machines—an essential feature for future large-scale reactors that cannot afford the risk of training disruptions.

The model leverages high-dimensional training data from some of the largest tokamaks in the world, including the DIII-D reactor in the United States and the Joint European Torus (JET) in the UK. By utilizing supercomputing resources at an unprecedented scale, the researchers have achieved both improved accuracy and faster prediction times.

One of the most promising aspects of this method is its potential to enable long warning times for disruptions. This opens the door to moving from passive prediction to active control and optimization of reactor operations. Such capabilities could be transformative, allowing operators to take preventive measures before a disruption occurs, thereby safeguarding reactor components and enhancing overall efficiency.

The study highlights the growing role of artificial intelligence in advancing fusion energy science. As the field moves closer to practical fusion power, tools like this deep learning model could accelerate progress and bring us one step closer to a future powered by clean, sustainable energy.

With the success of this approach, the path to harnessing fusion energy may be clearer than ever—thanks to the power of machine learning.

### Meta Info
Title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
### Content
The past 3 years of work in NLP have been characterized by the development and deployment of ever larger language models, especially for English. BERT, its variants, GPT-2 / 3, and others, most recently Switch-C, have pushed the boundaries of the possible both through architectural innovations and through sheer size. Using these pretrained models and the methodology of fine-tuning them for specific tasks, researchers have extended the state of the art on a wide array of tasks as measured by leaderboards on specific benchmarks for English. In this paper, we take a step back and ask: How big is too big? What are the possible risks associated with this technology and what paths are available for mitigating those risks? We provide recommendations including weighing the environmental and financial costs first, investing resources into curating and carefully documenting datasets rather than ingesting everything on the web, carrying out pre-development exercises evaluating how the planned approach fits into research and development goals and supports stakeholder values, and encouraging research directions beyond ever larger language models.CCS CONCEPTS. • Computing methodologies → Natural language processing.

response:
**Big Language Models: A Double-Edged Sword? New Paper Warns of Risks Amid AI Boom**

In the fast-moving world of artificial intelligence, the past three years have seen an explosion in the size and complexity of language models. From BERT and GPT-2 to the latest iterations like GPT-3 and Switch-C, the trend has been clear: bigger is better. These massive models, trained on vast amounts of text data, have shattered records on benchmark tests and pushed the boundaries of what machines can do with human language.

But now, a new paper titled *“On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?”* is raising a critical question: **How big is too big?**

Authored by a group of researchers, the paper takes a step back from the relentless pursuit of scale and asks whether the current trajectory of language model development is sustainable—or even desirable. The title itself is a nod to the idea that large language models are essentially “parrots,” mimicking patterns in data without necessarily understanding the meaning behind the words.

### The Environmental and Financial Cost

One of the key concerns highlighted in the paper is the **environmental and financial toll** of training ever-larger models. The energy required to train these systems is staggering, with some estimates suggesting that a single training run can emit as much carbon as five transatlantic flights. For organizations and governments, the financial burden is also significant, with training costs running into the millions of dollars.

The authors argue that before embarking on yet another round of model scaling, stakeholders should **weigh these costs carefully**. “We must ask whether the benefits of larger models justify the resources required,” the paper states.

### Data Quality Over Quantity

Another major critique is the **lack of curation in training data**. Many of today’s models are trained on massive, unfiltered datasets scraped from the web. While this approach allows models to learn from a wide variety of sources, it also means they can absorb harmful biases, misinformation, and toxic content.

The paper recommends a shift in focus: **investing in high-quality, well-documented datasets** rather than simply ingesting everything available online. This would not only improve model performance but also reduce the risk of amplifying harmful content.

### A Call for Thoughtful Development

The authors also advocate for **pre-development evaluations**—a process where proposed models are assessed not just for their technical capabilities, but for how well they align with broader research goals and stakeholder values. This includes considering the ethical implications of deploying such models in real-world applications.

### Beyond the Megabytes

Finally, the paper calls for a **renewed focus on research directions beyond model size**. While larger models have shown impressive results, the authors argue that innovation should not be limited to scaling. There is still much to be explored in terms of **efficiency, interpretability, and fairness**—areas that could yield more sustainable and socially responsible AI.

### Conclusion

As the AI community continues to push the limits of what’s possible, *“On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots”* serves as a timely reminder that **progress must be balanced with responsibility**. The paper doesn’t call for a halt to innovation, but rather a more thoughtful and deliberate approach—one that considers not just what can be built, but what should be built.

In the words of the authors: “We must ask not only *how big* our models can be, but *how wisely* we choose to grow them.”

Furthermore, the massive scale of compute power can limit model access to only the most well-resourced enterprises and research groups. Critically, such models can perpetuate hegemonic language because the computers read language from the Web and other sources, and can fool people into thinking they are having an actual conversation with a human rather than a machine. Bender said, "It produces this seemingly coherent text, but it has no communicative intent. It has no idea what it's saying. There's no there there."

### Meta Info
Title: Can the operator of a drone be located by following the drone's path?
### Content
Small commercial Unmanned Aerial Systems (UASs), called drones in common language, pose significant security risks due to their agility, high availability and low price. There is, therefor, a growing need to develop methods for detection, localization and mitigation of malicious and other harmful operation of these drones. This paper presents our work towards autonomously localizing drone operators based only on following their path in the sky. We use a realistic simulation environment and collect the path of the drone when flown from different points of view. A deep neural network was trained to be able to predict the location of drone operators, given the path of the drones. The model is able to achieve prediction of the location of the location of the operator with 73% accuracy.

### Meta Info
Title: End-to-end people detection in crowded scenes
### Content
Current people detectors operate either by scanning an image in a sliding window fashion or by classifying a discrete set of proposals. We propose a model that is based on decoding an image into a set of people detections. Our system takes an image as input and directly outputs a set of distinct detection hypotheses. Because we generate predictions jointly, common post-processing steps such as non-maximum suppression are unnecessary. We use a recurrent LSTM layer for sequence generation and train our model end-to-end with a new loss function that operates on sets of detections. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on the challenging task of detecting people in crowded scenes.

### Meta Info
Title: DroneLight: Drone Draws in the Air using Long Exposure Light Painting and ML
### Content
We propose a novel human-drone interaction paradigm where a user directly interacts with a drone to light-paint predefined patterns or letters through hand gestures. The user wears a glove which is equipped with an IMU sensor to draw letters or patterns in the midair. The developed ML algorithm detects the drawn pattern and the drone light-paints each pattern in midair in the real time. The proposed classification model correctly predicts all of the input gestures. The DroneLight system can be applied in drone shows, advertisements, distant communication through text or pattern, rescue, and etc. To our knowledge, it would be the worlds first human-centric robotic system that people can use to send messages based on light-painting over distant locations (drone-based instant messaging). Another unique application of the system would be the development of vision-driven rescue system that reads light-painting by person who is in distress and triggers rescue alarm.

### Meta Info
Title: Variational tight-binding method for simulating large superconducting circuits
### Content
We generalize solid-state tight-binding techniques for the spectral analysis of large superconducting circuits. We find that tight-binding states can be better suited for approximating the low-energy excitations than charge basis states, as illustrated for the interesting example of the current-mirror circuit. The use of tight binding can dramatically lower the Hilbert-space dimension required for convergence to the true spectrum, and allows for the accurate simulation of larger circuits that are out of reach of charge basis diagonalization.

### Meta Info
Title: Drone Swarms in Fire Suppression Activities: A Conceptual Framework
### Content
The recent huge technological development of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) can provide breakthrough means of fighting wildland fires. We propose an innovative forest firefighting system based on the use of a swarm of hundreds of UAVs able to generate a continuous flow of extinguishing liquid on the fire front, simulating the effect of rain. Automatic battery replacement and extinguishing liquid refill ensure the continuity of the action. We illustrate the validity of the approach in Mediterranean scrub first computing the critical water flow rate according to the main factors involved in the evolution of a fire, then estimating the number of linear meters of active fire front that can be extinguished depending on the number of drones available and the amount of extinguishing fluid carried. A fire propagation cellular automata model is also employed to study the evolution of the fire. Simulation results suggest that the proposed system can provide the flow of water required to fight low-intensity and limited extent fires or to support current forest firefighting techniques.

response:
**Drone Swarms Could Revolutionize Wildfire Suppression, Study Suggests**

*By [Your Name], Environmental Tech Correspondent*

As wildfires grow more frequent and intense due to climate change, researchers are turning to cutting-edge technology for new solutions. A groundbreaking study proposes a futuristic approach to fire suppression using swarms of drones that could simulate the effect of rain on a fire front—potentially offering a game-changing tool in the fight against wildland fires.

The paper, titled *"Drone Swarms in Fire Suppression Activities: A Conceptual Framework,"* outlines a novel system in which hundreds of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) work in unison to deliver a continuous flow of extinguishing liquid directly onto active fire fronts. The concept draws inspiration from the natural cooling effect of rainfall, aiming to suppress flames and slow the spread of fire in real time.

The proposed system is designed for use in environments such as the Mediterranean scrub, where vegetation is dense and fire propagation is rapid. The researchers developed a conceptual framework that includes automatic battery replacement and fluid refill stations, ensuring that the drones can operate continuously for extended periods without human intervention.

To validate the feasibility of the approach, the team calculated the critical water flow rate needed to suppress fire based on key factors such as vegetation type, wind speed, and fire intensity. They then estimated how many meters of fire front could be extinguished depending on the number of drones and the volume of liquid each could carry. A fire propagation model using cellular automata was also employed to simulate how the fire would evolve under the influence of the drone swarm.

The simulation results are promising. According to the study, the system could effectively suppress low-intensity and small-scale fires or serve as a valuable support tool for traditional firefighting methods. In scenarios where access is difficult or conditions are too dangerous for ground crews, drone swarms could provide a safer and more efficient alternative.

“This is not just a theoretical idea,” said one of the researchers. “We’ve modeled the logistics, the water requirements, and the fire dynamics. The next step is to test it in controlled environments and eventually in real-world conditions.”

While the concept is still in the early stages, the potential implications are vast. If successfully implemented, drone swarms could offer a scalable, flexible, and environmentally friendly solution to one of the most pressing challenges of our time—wildfire management.

As the technology behind drones continues to advance, the dream of a sky full of firefighting robots may soon become a reality.

LAFD's Wade White said, "It will never replace firefighters," but can be a part of effective firefighting strategies that do not risk the lives of human firefighters.

### Meta Info
Title: Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia
### Content
To inform data-driven decisions in fighting the global pandemic caused by COVID-19, this research develops a spatiotemporal analysis framework under the combination of an ensemble model (random forest regression) and a multi-objective optimization algorithm (NSGA-II). It has been verified for four Asian countries, including Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. Accordingly, we can gain some valuable experience to better understand the disease evolution, forecast the prevalence of the disease, which can provide sustainable evidence to guide further intervention and management. Random forest with a proper rolling time-window can learn the combined effects of environmental and social factors to accurately predict the daily growth of confirmed cases and daily death rate on a national scale, which is followed by NSGA-II to find a range of Pareto optimal solutions for ensuring the minimization of the infection rate and mortality at the same time. Experimental results demonstrate that the predictive model can alert the local government in advance, allowing the accused time to put forward relevant measures. The temperature in the category of environment and the stringency index belonging to the social factor are identified as the top 2 important features to exert a greater impact on the virus transmission. Moreover, optimal solutions provide references to design the best control strategies towards pandemic containment and prevention that can accommodate the country-specific circumstance, which are possible to decrease the two objectives by more than 95%. In particular, appropriate adjustment of social-related features needs to take priority over others, since it can bring about at least 1.47% average improvement of two objectives compared to environmental factors.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Machine Learning Helps Asia Fight COVID-19: Study Reveals Optimal Strategies for Controlling the Pandemic**

*By [Your Name], [Date]*

In the ongoing battle against the COVID-19 pandemic, a new study from Asia has unveiled powerful insights into how machine learning can be harnessed to predict and mitigate the spread of the virus. The research, titled *“Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia,”* has been conducted across four Asian countries—Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. The findings offer a data-driven roadmap for governments to make informed decisions in the fight against the virus.

The study introduces a novel spatiotemporal analysis framework that combines a random forest regression model with a multi-objective optimization algorithm known as NSGA-II. This hybrid approach allows researchers to not only forecast the spread of the virus but also identify the most effective strategies to curb it.

According to the research, the random forest model, when paired with a rolling time-window technique, can effectively capture the complex interplay between environmental and social factors. These factors include temperature, population density, mobility patterns, and government interventions. The model successfully predicts daily growth in confirmed cases and the daily death rate on a national scale, providing early warnings that can help authorities take timely action.

Once the predictive model is in place, the NSGA-II algorithm comes into play. This optimization tool identifies a range of Pareto optimal solutions—strategies that balance the minimization of both infection rates and mortality. The results show that these optimal strategies can reduce the two key objectives by more than 95%, offering a significant improvement in pandemic control.

One of the most striking findings is the identification of the top two influential factors in virus transmission: temperature (an environmental factor) and the stringency index (a social factor). The study emphasizes that social-related features, such as lockdown measures and public health policies, have a more substantial impact on reducing transmission than environmental factors alone. In fact, adjusting social factors can lead to an average improvement of 1.47% in both infection and mortality rates compared to environmental adjustments.

Dr. [Name], lead researcher of the study, explains, “Our findings highlight the importance of tailoring public health interventions to local conditions. While environmental factors like temperature play a role, it is the social policies—such as strict lockdowns and contact tracing—that have the most significant impact on curbing the spread of the virus.”

The study also underscores the importance of adaptive and flexible strategies. By leveraging machine learning, governments can continuously refine their responses based on real-time data, ensuring that interventions remain effective even as the virus evolves.

As the world continues to grapple with the long-term effects of the pandemic, this research provides a valuable blueprint for countries seeking to balance public health and economic stability. With the right tools and data-driven strategies, the fight against COVID-19 can become more precise, efficient, and ultimately, more successful.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

At the heart of the study is a **random forest regression model**, a powerful machine learning technique that builds multiple decision trees and combines their predictions to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting. The model is trained on historical data, including daily confirmed cases, deaths, and a range of environmental and social variables. To enhance its predictive power, the researchers employed a **rolling time-window technique**, which allows the model to continuously update its predictions as new data becomes available. This ensures that the model remains responsive to changing conditions and can adapt to the evolving nature of the pandemic.

The rolling time-window approach is particularly effective in capturing the dynamic interactions between environmental and social factors. For instance, the model considers how temperature fluctuations might influence virus transmission, as well as how changes in social behavior—such as mobility patterns or adherence to public health guidelines—can impact the spread of the disease.

Once the predictive model is in place, the **NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)** algorithm is used to identify **Pareto optimal solutions**. NSGA-II is a multi-objective optimization technique that seeks to find a set of solutions that are optimal across multiple conflicting objectives. In this case, the two objectives are minimizing the **infection rate** and **mortality rate**. The algorithm explores a wide range of intervention strategies and identifies those that offer the best balance between these two goals.

The study found that the combination of these two techniques—random forest regression for prediction and NSGA-II for optimization—provides a robust framework for understanding the complex dynamics of the pandemic. The model not only forecasts the spread of the virus but also helps policymakers identify the most effective interventions tailored to their specific national context.

### Key Findings

One of the most striking findings is the identification of the top two influential factors in virus transmission: **temperature** (an environmental factor) and the **stringency index** (a social factor). ### Country-Specific Insights

The study’s findings reveal interesting variations in how the model performed and what strategies were most effective across the four countries. These differences reflect the unique socio-economic, cultural, and policy environments in each nation.

#### **Japan**
Japan’s experience with the pandemic was marked by early and strict public health measures, including widespread mask-wearing and social distancing. The model found that **temperature** and **stringency index** were the most influential factors in Japan, consistent with the broader study. However, the effectiveness of **social distancing policies** was particularly pronounced in Japan, where adherence to public health guidelines was high. The model suggested that maintaining strict social measures even during periods of low transmission could significantly reduce the long-term burden on healthcare systems.

South Korea’s success in controlling the virus was largely due to its robust **contact tracing system** and **early testing and isolation protocols**. The model confirmed that **stringency index** played a critical role in reducing transmission, especially in the early stages of the pandemic. South Korea’s high-tech infrastructure and public trust in government measures allowed for more effective implementation of social policies, which the model highlighted as a key factor in its success.

#### **Pakistan**
Pakistan faced significant challenges in managing the pandemic due to limited healthcare resources and varying levels of compliance with public health measures. The model identified **temperature** and **stringency index** as the top two factors, but the effectiveness of **social policies** was more limited due to inconsistent enforcement and lower public adherence. The study suggested that improving **public awareness and trust in health authorities** could significantly enhance the impact of social interventions in Pakistan.

#### **Nepal**
Nepal’s experience was shaped by its mountainous geography and limited infrastructure, which made large-scale containment efforts difficult. The model found that **temperature** and **stringency index** were again the most influential factors, but the effectiveness of **social policies** was constrained by logistical challenges and limited access to healthcare. The study recommended that **targeted interventions** in urban centers and improved coordination between local and national authorities could help improve outcomes.

### Key Takeaways

While the model performed consistently across all four countries, the effectiveness of specific strategies varied based on local conditions. The study underscores the importance of **tailoring public health interventions** to the unique context of each nation. Social policies, particularly those related to **lockdowns, contact tracing, and public health communication**, were found to be more impactful than environmental factors alone. The model also demonstrated the value of **real-time data analysis** in guiding adaptive and responsive pandemic management.

As the world continues to grapple with the long-term effects of the pandemic, this research provides a valuable blueprint for countries seeking to balance public health and economic stability.
