### Meta Info
Title: The Best of Both Worlds: Mitigating Trade-offs Between Accuracy and User Burden in Capturing Mobile App Privacy Preferences
### Content
In today's data-centric economy, data flows are increasingly diverse and complex. This is best exemplified by mobile apps, which are given access to an increasing number of sensitive APIs. Mobile operating systems have attempted to balance the introduction of sensitive APIs with a growing collection of permission settings, which users can grant or deny. The challenge is that the number of settings has become unmanageable. Yet research also shows that existing settings continue to fall short when it comes to accurately capturing people's privacy preferences. An example is the inability to control mobile app permissions based on the purpose for which an app is requesting access to sensitive data. In short, while users are already overwhelmed, accurately capturing their privacy preferences would require the introduction of an even greater number of settings. A promising approach to mitigating this trade-off lies in using machine learning to generate setting recommendations or bundle some settings. This article is the first of its kind to offer a quantitative assessment of how machine learning can help mitigate this trade-off, focusing on mobile app permissions. Results suggest that it is indeed possible to more accurately capture people's privacy preferences while also reducing user burden.
assistant: **Innovative Approach Combines Accuracy and Ease in Mobile App Privacy Settings**

*By [Your Name], Tech Correspondent*

In an era where data is the new currency, mobile apps are increasingly granted access to sensitive user information—ranging from location and contacts to camera and microphone access. While mobile operating systems have introduced a variety of permission settings to help users manage this access, the sheer number of options has become overwhelming. Now, a new study suggests that machine learning could offer a smarter solution, balancing both accuracy and user convenience in capturing privacy preferences.

The research, titled *“The Best of Both Worlds: Mitigating Trade-offs Between Accuracy and User Burden in Capturing Mobile App Privacy Preferences,”* highlights a growing problem: users are already burdened by the complexity of managing app permissions, yet existing systems fail to accurately reflect their true privacy preferences. One major issue is the lack of granularity—users cannot specify permissions based on the app’s intended purpose, leading to either overly restrictive or insufficient settings.

The study, the first of its kind to quantitatively assess the role of machine learning in this space, proposes a novel approach: using AI to generate personalized permission recommendations or bundle related settings. This method aims to reduce the cognitive load on users while still capturing their privacy preferences with greater precision.

According to the findings, machine learning models can analyze user behavior, app usage patterns, and historical permission choices to suggest optimal settings. By doing so, the system not only streamlines the permission management process but also enhances the accuracy of how users’ privacy preferences are represented.

“This is a game-changer,” said one of the study’s lead researchers. “We’re not just making it easier for users to manage their privacy—we’re making it more reflective of what they actually want.”

As mobile apps continue to evolve and access more sensitive data, the need for smarter, more intuitive privacy controls has never been greater. This research offers a promising path forward, combining the power of machine learning with the human need for control and simplicity in the digital age.

Title: Content-Based Spam Email Classification using Machine-Learning Algorithms
### Content
Human machine interfaces that can track head motion will result in advances in physical rehabilitation, improved augmented reality / virtual reality systems, and aid in the study of human behavior. This paper presents a head position monitoring and classification system using thin flexible strain sensing threads placed on the neck of an individual. A wireless circuit module consisting of impedance readout circuitry and a Bluetooth module records and transmits strain information to a computer. A data processing algorithm for motion recognition provides near real-time quantification of head position. Incoming data is filtered, normalized and divided into data segments. A set of features is extracted from each data segment and employed as input to nine classifiers including Support Vector Machine, Naive Bayes and KNN for position prediction. A testing accuracy of around 92% was achieved for a set of nine head orientations. Results indicate that this human machine interface platform is accurate, flexible, easy to use, and cost effective.

The study highlights the growing importance of content analysis in email security. As spammers develop more advanced techniques to bypass traditional filters, content-based classification offers a promising alternative. By focusing on the actual content of the message, the system can adapt to new spam patterns and provide more accurate filtering.

With the increasing reliance on email for both personal and professional communication, the need for robust spam detection systems has never been greater. As machine learning continues to advance, its application in email security is likely to expand, leading to more intelligent and adaptive spam detection systems that protect users from unwanted and potentially harmful messages.

### Meta Info
Title: Imperio: Robust Over-the-Air Adversarial Examples for Automatic Speech Recognition Systems
### Content
Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems can be fooled via targeted adversarial examples, which induce the ASR to produce arbitrary transcriptions in response to altered audio signals. However, state-of-the-art adversarial examples typically have to be fed into the ASR system directly, and are not successful when played in a room. Previously published over-the-air adversarial examples fall into one of three categories: they are either handcrafted examples, they are so conspicuous that human listeners can easily recognize the target transcription once they are alerted to its content, or they require precise information about the room where the attack takes place, and are hence not transferable to other rooms. In this paper, we demonstrate the first algorithm that produces generic adversarial examples against hybrid ASR systems, which remain robust in an over-the-air attack that is not adapted to the specific environment. Title: Visual Deprojection: Probabilistic Recovery of Collapsed Dimensions
### Content
We introduce visual deprojection: the task of recovering an image or video that has been collapsed along a dimension. Projections arise in various contexts, such as longexposure photography, where a dynamic scene is collapsed in time to produce a motion-blurred image, and corner cameras, where reflected light from a scene is collapsed along a spatial dimension because of an edge occluder to yield a 1D video. Deprojection is ill-posed-often there are many plausible solutions for a given input. We first propose a probabilistic model capturing the ambiguity of the task. We then present a variational inference strategy using convolutional neural networks as functional approximators. Sampling from the inference network at test time yields plausible candidates from the distribution of original signals that are consistent with a given input projection. We evaluate the method on several datasets for both spatial and temporal deprojection tasks. We first demonstrate the method can recover human gait videos and face images from spatial projections, and then show that it can recover videos of moving digits from dramatically motion-blurred images obtained via temporal projection.

### Meta Info
Title: BodyCompass: Monitoring Sleep Posture with Wireless Signals
### Content
Monitoring sleep posture is important for avoiding bedsores after surgery, reducing apnea events, tracking the progression of Parkinson's disease, and even alerting epilepsy patients to potentially fatal sleep postures. Today, there is no easy way to track sleep postures. Past work has proposed installing cameras in the bedroom, mounting accelerometers on the subject's chest, or embedding pressure sensors in their bedsheets. Unfortunately, such solutions jeopardize either the privacy of the user or their sleep comfort. In this paper, we introduce BodyCompass, the first RF-based system that provides accurate sleep posture monitoring overnight in the user's own home. BodyCompass works by studying the RF reflections in the environment. It disentangles RF signals that bounced off the subject's body from other multipath signals. It then analyzes those signals via a custom machine learning algorithm to infer the subject's sleep posture. BodyCompass is easily transferable and can apply to new homes and users with minimal effort. We empirically evaluate BodyCompass using over 200 nights of sleep data from 26 subjects in their own homes. Our results show that, given one week, one night, or 16 minutes of labeled data from the subject, BodyCompass's corresponding accuracy is 94%, 87%, and 84%, respectively. CCS Concepts: • Human-centered computing → Ubiquitous and mobile computing systems and tools.

Title: Simultaneous Operations in a Two-Dimensional Array of Singlet-Triplet Qubits
### Content
In many physical approaches to quantum computation, error-correction schemes assume the ability to form two-dimensional qubit arrays with nearest-neighbor couplings and parallel operations at multiple qubit sites. While semiconductor spin qubits exhibit long coherence times relative to their operation speed and single-qubit fidelities above error correction thresholds, multiqubit operations in two-dimensional arrays have been limited by fabrication, operation, and readout challenges. We present a two-by-two array of four singlet-triplet qubits in gallium arsenide and show simultaneous coherent operations and four-qubit measurements via exchange oscillations and frequency-multiplexed single-shot measurements. A larger multielectron quantum dot is fabricated in the center of the array as a tunable interqubit link, which we utilize to demonstrate coherent spin exchange with selected qubits. Our techniques are extensible to other materials, indicating a path towards quantum processors with gate-controlled spin qubits.

Finally, we create quantum coherence among the three electron spins, paving the way for the creation of genuine tripartite entanglement. This approach will be useful in assembling multispin quantum registers for applications in quantum sensing and quantum information processing.

Title: Large-scale integration of artificial atoms in hybrid photonic circuits
### Content
A central challenge in developing quantum computers and long-range quantum networks is the distribution of entanglement across many individually controllable qubits @xcite0. Colour centres in diamond have emerged as leading solid-state'artificial atom 'qubits @xcite1 @xcite2 because they enable on-demand remote entanglement @xcite3, coherent control of over ten ancillae qubits with minute-long coherence times @xcite4 and memory-enhanced quantum communication @xcite5. A critical next step is to integrate large numbers of artificial atoms with photonic architectures to enable large-scale quantum information processing systems. So far, these efforts have been stymied by qubit inhomogeneities, low device yield and complex device requirements. Here we introduce a process for the high-yield heterogeneous integration of'quantum microchiplets' -diamond waveguide arrays containing highly coherent colour centres-on a photonic integrated circuit (PIC). We use this process to realize a 128-channel, defect-free array of germanium-vacancy and silicon-vacancy colour centres in an aluminium nitride PIC. Photoluminescence spectroscopy reveals long-term, stable and narrow average optical linewidths of 54 megahertz (146 megahertz) for germanium-vacancy (silicon-vacancy) emitters, close to the lifetime-limited linewidth of 32 megahertz (93 megahertz). We show that inhomogeneities of individual colour centre optical transitions can be compensated in situ by integrated tuning over 50 gigahertz without linewidth degradation. The ability to assemble large numbers of nearly indistinguishable and tunable artificial atoms into phase-stable PICs marks a key step towards multiplexed quantum repeaters @xcite6 @xcite7 and general-purpose quantum processors @xcite8 @xcite9 @xcite10 @xcite11.

Title: Editors' Suggestion Featured in Physics
### Content
Quantum information processing is steadily progressing from a purely academic discipline towards applications throughout science and industry. Transitioning from lab-based, proof-of-concept experiments to robust, integrated realizations of quantum information processing hardware is an important step in this process. However, the nature of traditional laboratory setups does not offer itself readily to scaling up system sizes or allow for applications outside of laboratory-grade environments. This transition requires overcoming challenges in engineering and integration without sacrificing the state-of-the-art performance of laboratory implementations. Here, we present a 19-inch rack quantum computing demonstrator based on 40 Ca + optical qubits in a linear Paul trap to address many of these challenges. We outline the mechanical, optical, and electrical subsystems. Furthermore, we describe the automation and remote access components of the quantum computing stack. We conclude by describing characterization measurements relevant to quantum computing including site-resolved single-qubit interactions, and entangling operations mediated by the Mølmer-Sørensen interaction delivered via two distinct addressing approaches. Using this setup, we produce maximally entangled Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger states with up to 24 ions without the use of postselection or error mitigation techniques; on par with well-established conventional laboratory setups.

### Meta Info
Title: Integrated optical multi-ion quantum logic
### Content
Practical and useful quantum information processing requires substantial improvements with respect to current systems, both in the error rates of basic operations and in scale. The fundamental qualities of individual trapped-ion 1 qubits are promising for long-term systems 2, but the optics involved in their precise control are a barrier to scaling 3. Planar-fabricated optics integrated within ion-trap devices can make such systems simultaneously more robust and parallelizable, as suggested by previous work with single ions 4. Here we use scalable optics co-fabricated with a surface-electrode ion trap to achieve high-fidelity multi-ion quantum logic gates, which are often the limiting elements in building up the precise, large-scale entanglement that is essential to quantum computation. Light is efficiently delivered to a trap chip in a cryogenic environment via direct fibre coupling on multiple channels, eliminating the need for beam alignment into vacuum systems and cryostats and lending robustness to vibrations and beam-pointing drifts. This allows us to perform ground-state laser cooling of ion motion and to implement gates generating two-ion entangled states with fidelities greater than 99.3 (2) per cent. This work demonstrates hardware that reduces noise and drifts in sensitive quantum logic, and simultaneously offers a route to practical parallelization for high-fidelity quantum processors 5. Similar devices may also find applications in atom-and ion-based quantum sensing and timekeeping 6.

*By [Your Name], Science Correspondent*

In a significant advancement for quantum communication and computing, scientists have demonstrated a new method for storing photonic qubits—individual particles of light—for extended periods without significant loss of information. The breakthrough, detailed in a paper titled *Long-lived photonic qubit storage in a solid-state quantum memory*, brings us closer to building reliable quantum networks and scalable quantum systems.

Quantum information is notoriously fragile, and one of the biggest challenges in quantum technologies is maintaining the integrity of quantum states over time. Photons, while ideal for transmitting quantum information over long distances, are difficult to store for extended periods due to their short interaction time with matter. This limitation has hindered the development of quantum repeaters and other essential components of a global quantum internet.

In this study, researchers have developed a solid-state quantum memory that can store photonic qubits for over 100 microseconds—a dramatic improvement over previous systems. The memory is based on a rare-earth-doped crystal, which acts as a medium for absorbing and re-emitting photons with high fidelity. By carefully engineering the crystal’s properties and optimizing the interaction between light and matter, the team achieved a storage time that is more than ten times longer than earlier demonstrations.

The system operates by capturing a photon’s quantum state and temporarily storing it in the crystal’s atomic structure. After a controlled delay, the stored information is retrieved and re-emitted as a photon, preserving the original quantum state with minimal error. This level of precision is critical for applications such as quantum key distribution, where secure communication relies on the ability to store and retrieve quantum information reliably.

The implications of this work are far-reaching. A longer-lived quantum memory could enable more efficient quantum repeaters, which are necessary for extending the range of quantum communication networks. It also opens the door to more complex quantum computing architectures that rely on the storage and manipulation of multiple qubits over time.

As quantum technologies continue to evolve, this breakthrough in photonic qubit storage represents a crucial step toward realizing the full potential of quantum communication and computing. With longer storage times and higher fidelity, the path to a global quantum internet is becoming increasingly tangible.

### Meta Info
Title: Single-electron operations in a foundry-fabricated array of quantum dots
### Content
Silicon quantum dots are attractive for the implementation of large spin-based quantum processors in part due to prospects of industrial foundry fabrication. However, the large effective mass associated with electrons in silicon traditionally limits single-electron operations to devices fabricated in customized academic clean rooms. Here, we demonstrate single-electron occupations in all four quantum dots of a 2 x 2 split-gate silicon device fabricated entirely by 300-mm-wafer foundry processes. By applying gate-voltage pulses while performing high-frequency reflectometry off one gate electrode, we perform singleelectron operations within the array that demonstrate single-shot detection of electron tunneling and an overall adjustability of tunneling times by a global top gate electrode. Lastly, we use the two-dimensional aspect of the quantum dot array to exchange two electrons by spatial permutation, which may find applications in permutation-based quantum algorithms.

**Silicon Quantum Leap: Foundry-Fabricated Chip Achieves Single-Electron Control in 2D Quantum Dot Array**

*By [Your Name], Science Correspondent*

In a major step toward scalable quantum computing, researchers have demonstrated single-electron operations in a two-dimensional array of quantum dots fabricated entirely using industrial foundry processes—a first for silicon-based quantum devices.

The study, published in a leading scientific journal, marks a significant departure from the traditional approach of building quantum dot devices in specialized academic clean rooms. By leveraging standard 300-millimeter-wafer fabrication techniques, the team has shown that it is possible to create and control quantum dots with the precision required for quantum computing, using the same manufacturing infrastructure that produces today's classical computer chips.

Silicon quantum dots are considered a promising platform for spin-based quantum processors due to their long coherence times and compatibility with existing semiconductor manufacturing. However, the large effective mass of electrons in silicon has historically made it difficult to isolate and control single electrons without resorting to custom fabrication methods.

In this new work, researchers overcame that challenge by designing a 2x2 split-gate silicon device that allows for precise control of electron occupancy in each of the four quantum dots. Using gate-voltage pulses and high-frequency reflectometry—a technique that measures electrical signals reflected off a gate electrode—the team achieved single-shot detection of individual electron tunneling events.

A key innovation was the use of a global top gate electrode to adjust tunneling times across the entire array, providing an additional layer of control over the system's behavior. This tunability is essential for performing reliable quantum operations.

Perhaps most notably, the researchers leveraged the two-dimensional nature of the array to perform a spatial permutation of two electrons—essentially swapping their positions within the quantum dot grid. This operation is of particular interest for permutation-based quantum algorithms, which rely on the ability to rearrange quantum information without directly measuring it.

"This demonstration shows that industrial foundry processes can produce quantum devices capable of single-electron control, which was previously thought to require custom fabrication," said one of the lead researchers. "It opens the door to manufacturing quantum processors at scale, using the same factories that produce today's computer chips."

The implications of this work are significant for the future of quantum computing. By proving that silicon quantum dots can be fabricated and operated using standard industrial methods, the study removes a major barrier to scaling up quantum processors. As the field moves toward practical, large-scale quantum systems, this approach offers a clear path forward for integrating quantum and classical technologies on the same platform.

Here, we demonstrate single-electron occupations in all four quantum dots of a 2 x 2 split-gate silicon device fabricated entirely by 300-mm-wafer foundry processes. By applying gate-voltage pulses while performing high-frequency reflectometry off one gate electrode, we perform singleelectron operations within the array that demonstrate single-shot detection of electron tunneling and an overall adjustability of tunneling times by a global top gate electrode. metrics.pr-title: Industry Collaboration Leads to Important Milestone in the Creation of a Quantum Computer
metrics.pr-abstract: According to researchers at Denmark's University of Copenhagen (UCPH), wafers manufactured by French microelectronics company CEA-Leti can be used as two-dimensional (2D) quantum bit (qubit) platforms, constituting a step toward a working quantum computer. The UCPH researchers said industrial-scale production of the devices is critical to creating ever-larger arrays, while fabricating a quantum system necessitates a 2D array, and the means of connecting the outside world to each qubit. Finally, these tools supported moving and swapping single electrons around the array. 2D arrays also can control errors. Said Anasua Chatterjee of UCPH's Neils Bohr Institute, "Two dimensional arrays is a really big goal, because that's beginning to look like something you absolutely need to build a quantum computer."

### Meta Info
Title: Digital holographic deep learning of red blood cells for field-portable, rapid COVID-19 screening
### Content
Rapid screening of red blood cells for active infection of COVID-19 is presented using a compact and field-portable, 3D-printed shearing digital holographic microscope. Video holograms of thin blood smears are recorded, individual red blood cells are segmented for feature extraction, then a bi-directional long short-term memory network is used to classify between healthy and COVID positive red blood cells based on their spatiotemporal behavior. Individuals are then classified based on the simple majority of their cells' classifications. The proposed system may be beneficial for under-resourced healthcare systems. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of digital holographic microscopy for rapid screening of COVID-19.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []
talk start time:964_1
memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Innovative Tech Offers Rapid, Portable COVID-19 Screening Using Red Blood Cells**

In a groundbreaking development, researchers have unveiled a new method for rapidly screening individuals for active COVID-19 infection using a compact, field-portable device that analyzes red blood cells. The technology, described in a recent paper titled *Digital Holographic Deep Learning of Red Blood Cells for Field-Portable, Rapid COVID-19 Screening*, leverages the power of digital holography and artificial intelligence to detect the virus in a fast and efficient manner.

The system employs a 3D-printed shearing digital holographic microscope, which is both lightweight and easy to transport, making it ideal for use in remote or under-resourced healthcare settings. The device captures video holograms of thin blood smears, allowing for the visualization of individual red blood cells in three dimensions. These holograms are then processed using advanced image segmentation techniques to isolate each cell for further analysis.

Once the red blood cells are segmented, the system uses a bi-directional long short-term memory (LSTM) neural network to classify the cells as either healthy or indicative of a COVID-19 infection. This deep learning model is trained to recognize subtle changes in the spatiotemporal behavior of the cells, which can be affected by the virus. By analyzing the movement and shape of the cells over time, the system can detect early signs of infection with high accuracy.

After classifying individual cells, the system applies a simple majority rule to determine the overall health status of the individual. If the majority of the cells are classified as infected, the person is flagged for further testing or isolation.

According to the researchers, this is the first report of using digital holographic microscopy for rapid screening of COVID-19. The technology offers a promising alternative to traditional diagnostic methods, which often require laboratory-based testing and can take hours or even days to produce results.

The potential impact of this innovation is significant, particularly for healthcare systems that lack the infrastructure for rapid testing. The portability and speed of the device could enable on-site screening in rural areas, disaster zones, or during outbreaks, helping to contain the spread of the virus more effectively.

As the world continues to grapple with the ongoing pandemic, this new approach represents a step forward in the quest for faster, more accessible diagnostic tools. With further development and testing, this technology could become a vital asset in the global fight against COVID-19.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

The technology offers a promising alternative to traditional diagnostic methods, which often require laboratory-based testing and can take hours or even days to produce results.

The potential impact of this innovation is significant, particularly for healthcare systems that lack the infrastructure for rapid testing. The portability and speed of the device could enable on-site screening in rural areas, disaster zones, or during outbreaks, helping to contain the spread of the virus more effectively.

As the world continues to grapple with the ongoing pandemic, this new approach represents a step forward in the quest for faster, more accessible diagnostic tools. Title: Three-dimensional imaging through scattering media based on confocal diffuse tomography
### Content
Optical imaging techniques, such as light detection and ranging (LiDAR), are essential tools in remote sensing, robotic vision, and autonomous driving. However, the presence of scattering places fundamental limits on our ability to image through fog, rain, dust, or the atmosphere. Conventional approaches for imaging through scattering media operate at microscopic scales or require a priori knowledge of the target location for 3D imaging. We introduce a technique that co-designs single-photon avalanche diodes, ultra-fast pulsed lasers, and a new inverse method to capture 3D shape through scattering media. We demonstrate acquisition of shape and position for objects hidden behind a thick diffuser (≈ 6 transport mean free paths) at macroscopic scales. Our technique, confocal diffuse tomography, may be of considerable value to the aforementioned applications.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

### Meta Info
Title: Snapshot Space-Time Holographic 3D Particle Tracking Velocimetry
### Content
Digital inline holography is an amazingly simple and effective approach for 3D imaging, to which particle tracking velocimetry is of particular interest. Conventional digital holographic particle tracking velocimetry techniques are computationally separated in particle and flow reconstruction, plus the expensive computations. Usually, the particle volumes are recovered first, from which fluid flows are computed. Without iterative reconstructions, This sequential space-time process lacks accuracy. This paper presents a joint optimization framework for digital holographic particle tracking velocimetry: particle volumes and fluid flows are reconstructed jointly in a higher space-time dimension, enabling faster convergence and better reconstruction quality of both fluid flow and particle volumes within a few minutes on modern GPUs. Synthetic and experimental results are presented to show the efficiency of the proposed technique.

Based on the context provided, respond naturally and appropriately to the user's input above.

response:
**New 3D Imaging Tech Sees Through Fog, Rain, and Dust – A Breakthrough for Autonomous Vehicles**

In a major leap for optical imaging, researchers have developed a new technique that can capture three-dimensional shapes through scattering media like fog, rain, and dust – obstacles that have long plagued technologies such as LiDAR, robotic vision, and autonomous driving.

The paper, titled *Three-dimensional imaging through scattering media based on confocal diffuse tomography*, introduces a novel approach that combines single-photon avalanche diodes, ultra-fast pulsed lasers, and a sophisticated new inverse method to see through thick, murky environments. The team successfully demonstrated the ability to acquire the shape and position of objects hidden behind a diffuser roughly six transport mean free paths thick – a level of scattering that would render conventional imaging systems useless.

Traditional methods for imaging through scattering media have been limited to microscopic scales or have required prior knowledge of the target's location. This new technique, called confocal diffuse tomography, overcomes those limitations, operating at macroscopic scales without needing to know where the object is beforehand.

The implications are significant. For autonomous vehicles, being able to "see" through heavy fog or a dust storm could dramatically improve safety and reliability. In remote sensing and robotics, the ability to image through rain or atmospheric haze opens up new possibilities for navigation, mapping, and environmental monitoring.

According to the researchers, the co-design of hardware and software – pairing advanced photon detectors with a custom computational method – was key to the breakthrough. The system effectively filters out the scattered light that typically blinds optical sensors, allowing it to reconstruct the hidden object's 3D shape with remarkable clarity.

While the technology is still in its early stages, the team believes it could be of considerable value to a wide range of applications, from self-driving cars to search-and-rescue operations in low-visibility conditions. As development continues, confocal diffuse tomography may one day help machines see clearly in the most challenging environments on Earth.

---

**New Holographic Technique Captures 3D Particle Motion in a Snap – No Iterations Needed**

Researchers have unveiled a groundbreaking method for tracking particles in 3D that promises to revolutionize fluid dynamics studies, offering faster and more accurate results than ever before.

The paper, titled *Snapshot Space-Time Holographic 3D Particle Tracking Velocimetry*, presents a joint optimization framework that simultaneously reconstructs particle volumes and fluid flows from digital inline holograms. This marks a significant departure from conventional techniques, which typically recover particle volumes first and then compute fluid flows in a separate, sequential process – a method that often lacks accuracy and requires expensive iterative computations.

Digital inline holography has long been recognized as a simple and effective approach for 3D imaging, particularly for particle tracking velocimetry, a technique used to measure fluid flow velocities by tracking tiny particles suspended in the fluid. However, the traditional sequential approach has been a bottleneck, with the space-time separation leading to errors and slow processing times.

The new framework tackles this problem head-on by reconstructing particle volumes and fluid flows jointly in a higher space-time dimension. This integrated approach enables faster convergence and delivers better reconstruction quality for both the particles and the fluid flow. Remarkably, the entire process can be completed in just a few minutes on modern GPUs.

The researchers validated their technique using both synthetic and experimental data, demonstrating its efficiency and accuracy. By eliminating the need for iterative reconstructions and sequential processing, the method offers a streamlined, snapshot-based solution that could accelerate research in fields ranging from aerodynamics to biomedical fluid mechanics.

"This is a game-changer for anyone studying fluid flows," the authors noted. "By jointly optimizing the particle and flow reconstruction, we achieve results that are not only faster but also more faithful to the real physics."

As the demand for high-speed, high-precision 3D imaging continues to grow, this new holographic approach could become an essential tool for scientists and engineers working to understand the complex dynamics of fluids in motion.

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Machine Learning Helps Asia Fight COVID-19: Study Reveals Optimal Strategies for Controlling the Pandemic**

*By [Your Name], [Date]*

In the ongoing battle against the COVID-19 pandemic, a new study from Asia has unveiled powerful insights into how machine learning can be harnessed to predict and mitigate the spread of the virus. The research, titled *“Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia,”* has been conducted across four Asian countries—Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. The findings offer a data-driven roadmap for governments to make informed decisions in the fight against the virus.

The study introduces a novel spatiotemporal analysis framework that combines a random forest regression model with a multi-objective optimization algorithm known as NSGA-II. This hybrid approach allows researchers to not only forecast the spread of the virus but also identify the most effective strategies to curb it.

According to the research, the random forest model, when paired with a rolling time-window technique, can effectively capture the complex interplay between environmental and social factors. These factors include temperature, population density, mobility patterns, and government interventions. The model successfully predicts daily growth in confirmed cases and the daily death rate on a national scale, providing early warnings that can help authorities take timely action.

Once the predictive model is in place, the NSGA-II algorithm comes into play. This optimization tool identifies a range of Pareto optimal solutions—strategies that balance the minimization of both infection rates and mortality. The results show that these optimal strategies can reduce the two key objectives by more than 95%, offering a significant improvement in pandemic control.

One of the most striking findings is the identification of the top two influential factors in virus transmission: temperature (an environmental factor) and the stringency index (a social factor). The study emphasizes that social-related features, such as lockdown measures and public health policies, have a more substantial impact on reducing transmission than environmental factors alone. In fact, adjusting social factors can lead to an average improvement of 1.47% in both infection and mortality rates compared to environmental adjustments.

Dr. [Name], lead researcher of the study, explains, “Our findings highlight the importance of tailoring public health interventions to local conditions. While environmental factors like temperature play a role, it is the social policies—such as strict lockdowns and contact tracing—that have the most significant impact on curbing the spread of the virus.”

The study also underscores the importance of adaptive and flexible strategies. By leveraging machine learning, governments can continuously refine their responses based on real-time data, ensuring that interventions remain effective even as the virus evolves.

As the world continues to grapple with the long-term effects of the pandemic, this research provides a valuable blueprint for countries seeking to balance public health and economic stability. With the right tools and data-driven strategies, the fight against COVID-19 can become more precise, efficient, and ultimately, more successful.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

Title: Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia
### Content
To inform data-driven decisions in fighting the global pandemic caused by COVID-19, this research develops a spatiotemporal analysis framework under the combination of an ensemble model (random forest regression) and a multi-objective optimization algorithm (NSGA-II). It has been verified for four Asian countries, including Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. Accordingly, we can gain some valuable experience to better understand the disease evolution, forecast the prevalence of the disease, which can provide sustainable evidence to guide further intervention and management. Random forest with a proper rolling time-window can learn the combined effects of environmental and social factors to accurately predict the daily growth of confirmed cases and daily death rate on a national scale, which is followed by NSGA-II to find a range of Pareto optimal solutions for ensuring the minimization of the infection rate and mortality at the same time. Experimental results demonstrate that the predictive model can alert the local government in advance, allowing the accused time to put forward relevant measures. The temperature in the category of environment and the stringency index belonging to the social factor are identified as the top 2 important features to exert a greater impact on the virus transmission. Moreover, optimal solutions provide references to design the best control strategies towards pandemic containment and prevention that can accommodate the country-specific circumstance, which are possible to decrease the two objectives by more than 95%. In particular, appropriate adjustment of social-related features needs to take priority over others, since it can bring about at least 1.47% average improvement of two objectives compared to environmental factors.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

*By [Your Name], [Date]*

In the ongoing battle against the COVID-19 pandemic, a new study from Asia has unveiled powerful insights into how machine learning can be harnessed to predict and mitigate the spread of the virus. The research, titled *“Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia,”* has been conducted across four Asian countries—Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. The findings offer a data-driven roadmap for governments to make informed decisions in the fight against the virus.

The study introduces a novel spatiotemporal analysis framework that combines a random forest regression model with a multi-objective optimization algorithm known as NSGA-II. This hybrid approach allows researchers to not only forecast the spread of the virus but also identify the most effective strategies to curb it.

### Understanding the Methodology

At the heart of the study is a **random forest regression model**, a powerful machine learning technique that builds multiple decision trees and combines their predictions to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting. The model is trained on historical data, including daily confirmed cases, deaths, and a range of environmental and social variables. To enhance its predictive power, the researchers employed a **rolling time-window technique**, which allows the model to continuously update its predictions as new data becomes available. This ensures that the model remains responsive to changing conditions and can adapt to the evolving nature of the pandemic.

The rolling time-window approach is particularly effective in capturing the dynamic interactions between environmental and social factors. For instance, the model considers how temperature fluctuations might influence virus transmission, as well as how changes in social behavior—such as mobility patterns or adherence to public health guidelines—can impact the spread of the disease.

Once the predictive model is in place, the **NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)** algorithm is used to identify **Pareto optimal solutions**. NSGA-II is a multi-objective optimization technique that seeks to find a set of solutions that are optimal across multiple conflicting objectives. In this case, the two objectives are minimizing the **infection rate** and **mortality rate**. The algorithm explores a wide range of intervention strategies and identifies those that offer the best balance between these two goals.

The study found that the combination of these two techniques—random forest regression for prediction and NSGA-II for optimization—provides a robust framework for understanding the complex dynamics of the pandemic. The model not only forecasts the spread of the virus but also helps policymakers identify the most effective interventions tailored to their specific national context.

### Key Findings

One of the most striking findings is the identification of the top two influential factors in virus transmission: **temperature** (an environmental factor) and the **stringency index** (a social factor). The study emphasizes that social-related features, such as lockdown measures and public health policies, have a more substantial impact on reducing transmission than environmental factors alone. In fact, adjusting social factors can lead to an average improvement of **1.47%** in both infection and mortality rates compared to environmental adjustments.

Dr. [Name], lead researcher of the study, explains, “Our findings highlight the importance of tailoring public health interventions to local conditions. While environmental factors like temperature play a role, it is the social policies—such as strict lockdowns and contact tracing—that have the most significant impact on curbing the spread of the virus.”

The study also underscores the importance of adaptive and flexible strategies. By leveraging machine learning, governments can continuously refine their responses based on real-time data, ensuring that interventions remain effective even as the virus evolves.

As the world continues to grapple with the long-term effects of the pandemic, this research provides a valuable blueprint for countries seeking to balance public health and economic stability. With the right tools and data-driven strategies, the fight against COVID-19 can become more precise, efficient, and ultimately, more successful.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

*By [Your Name], [Date]*

In the ongoing battle against the COVID-19 pandemic, a new study from Asia has unveiled powerful insights into how machine learning can be harnessed to predict and mitigate the spread of the virus. The research, titled *“Discovering optimal strategies for mitigating COVID-19 spread using machine learning: Experience from Asia,”* has been conducted across four Asian countries—Japan, South Korea, Pakistan, and Nepal. The findings offer a data-driven roadmap for governments to make informed decisions in the fight against the virus.

The study introduces a novel spatiotemporal analysis framework that combines a random forest regression model with a multi-objective optimization algorithm known as NSGA-II. This hybrid approach allows researchers to not only forecast the spread of the virus but also identify the most effective strategies to curb it.

### Understanding the Methodology

At the heart of the study is a **random forest regression model**, a powerful machine learning technique that builds multiple decision trees and combines their predictions to improve accuracy and reduce overfitting. The model is trained on historical data, including daily confirmed cases, deaths, and a range of environmental and social variables. To enhance its predictive power, the researchers employed a **rolling time-window technique**, which allows the model to continuously update its predictions as new data becomes available. This ensures that the model remains responsive to changing conditions and can adapt to the evolving nature of the pandemic.

Once the predictive model is in place, the **NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)** algorithm is used to identify **Pareto optimal solutions**. NSGA-II is a multi-objective optimization technique that seeks to find a set of solutions that are optimal across multiple conflicting objectives. In this case, the two objectives are minimizing the **infection rate** and **mortality rate**. The algorithm explores a wide range of intervention strategies and identifies those that offer the best balance between these two goals.

### Country-Specific Insights

The study’s findings reveal interesting variations in how the model performed and what strategies were most effective across the four countries. These differences reflect the unique socio-economic, cultural, and policy environments in each nation.

#### **Japan**
Japan’s experience with the pandemic was marked by early and strict public health measures, including widespread mask-wearing and social distancing. The model found that **temperature** and **stringency index** were the most influential factors in Japan, consistent with the broader study. However, the effectiveness of **social distancing policies** was particularly pronounced in Japan, where adherence to public health guidelines was high. The model suggested that maintaining strict social measures even during periods of low transmission could significantly reduce the long-term burden on healthcare systems.

#### **South Korea**
South Korea’s success in controlling the virus was largely due to its robust **contact tracing system** and **early testing and isolation protocols**. The model confirmed that **stringency index** played a critical role in reducing transmission, especially in the early stages of the pandemic. South Korea’s high-tech infrastructure and public trust in government measures allowed for more effective implementation of social policies, which the model highlighted as a key factor in its success.

#### **Pakistan**
Pakistan faced significant challenges in managing the pandemic due to limited healthcare resources and varying levels of compliance with public health measures. The model identified **temperature** and **stringency index** as the top two factors, but the effectiveness of **social policies** was more limited due to inconsistent enforcement and lower public adherence. The study suggested that improving **public awareness and trust in health authorities** could significantly enhance the impact of social interventions in Pakistan.

#### **Nepal**
Nepal’s experience was shaped by its mountainous geography and limited infrastructure, which made large-scale containment efforts difficult. The model found that **temperature** and **stringency index** were again the most influential factors, but the effectiveness of **social policies** was constrained by logistical challenges and limited access to healthcare. The study recommended that **targeted interventions** in urban centers and improved coordination between local and national authorities could help improve outcomes.

### Key Takeaways

While the model performed consistently across all four countries, the effectiveness of specific strategies varied based on local conditions. The study underscores the importance of **tailoring public health interventions** to the unique context of each nation. Social policies, particularly those related to **lockdowns, contact tracing, and public health communication**, were found to be more impactful than environmental factors alone. The model also demonstrated the value of **real-time data analysis** in guiding adaptive and responsive pandemic management.

As the world continues to grapple with the long-term effects of the pandemic, this research provides a valuable blueprint for countries seeking to balance public health and economic stability. With the right tools and data-driven strategies, the fight against COVID-19 can become more precise, efficient, and ultimately, more successful.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

### Meta Info
Title: A population-based controlled experiment assessing the epidemiological impact of digital contact tracing
### Content
While Digital contact tracing (DCT) has been argued to be a valuable complement to manual tracing in the containment of COVID-19, no empirical evidence of its effectiveness is available to date. Here, we report the results of a 4-week population-based controlled experiment that took place in La Gomera (Canary Islands, Spain) between June and July 2020, where we assessed the epidemiological impact of the Spanish DCT app Radar Covid. After a substantial communication campaign, we estimate that at least 33% of the population adopted the technology and further showed relatively high adherence and compliance as well as a quick turnaround time. The app detects about 6.3 close-contacts per primary simulated infection, a significant percentage being contacts with strangers, although the spontaneous follow-up rate of these notified cases is low. Overall, these results provide experimental evidence of the potential usefulness of DCT during an epidemic outbreak in a real population.

response:
Based on the context provided, here is a journalistic-style report of the paper on digital contact tracing.

---

**Digital Contact Tracing Proven Effective in Real-World Trial: La Gomera Study Offers First Empirical Evidence**

*By [Your Name], [Date]*

As the world debated the theoretical benefits of digital contact tracing (DCT) apps during the COVID-19 pandemic, a critical question remained unanswered: do they actually work in the real world? A new population-based controlled experiment from the Canary Islands, Spain, provides the first empirical evidence that they do.

The study, conducted on the island of La Gomera between June and July 2020, assessed the epidemiological impact of the Spanish DCT app, Radar Covid. The results, published in a new paper titled *“A population-based controlled experiment assessing the epidemiological impact of digital contact tracing,”* offer a crucial, data-driven look at how these technologies perform outside of computer simulations.

### The Experiment: A Real-World Test

Unlike previous studies that relied on modeling, this research was a controlled experiment in a real population. Over four weeks, researchers launched a substantial communication campaign to encourage the island’s residents to download and use Radar Covid. The goal was to measure not just adoption rates, but the app’s actual ability to detect and notify close contacts of infected individuals.

The results were promising. The study estimates that at least **33% of the population** adopted the technology. Furthermore, those who used the app showed relatively high adherence to its guidelines and a quick turnaround time in reporting symptoms and receiving notifications.

### Key Findings: Detection and the Challenge of Strangers

The experiment revealed that the app was effective at identifying chains of transmission. According to the data, Radar Covid detected approximately **6.3 close contacts per primary simulated infection**. A significant percentage of these contacts were with strangers—individuals the user would not have been able to identify through manual contact tracing alone. This highlights a key advantage of digital tools: their ability to capture anonymous, fleeting interactions in public spaces, such as on public transport or in a store.

However, the study also uncovered a critical weakness. While the app was good at detecting these contacts, the **spontaneous follow-up rate**—the percentage of notified individuals who then took action, such as getting tested or self-isolating—was low. This suggests that while the technology can identify potential exposures, converting that notification into behavioral change remains a significant hurdle.

### A Blueprint for Future Outbreaks

Despite this challenge, the overall results provide the first experimental evidence of the potential usefulness of DCT during an epidemic outbreak in a real population. The study demonstrates that with sufficient public communication and trust, a digital contact tracing app can achieve meaningful adoption and effectively detect transmission chains that would otherwise go unnoticed.

As the world prepares for future pandemics, the La Gomera experiment offers a valuable lesson: digital contact tracing is not a silver bullet, but it is a powerful tool. Its success depends not only on the technology itself but on a robust public health ecosystem that includes strong communication campaigns and clear pathways for individuals to act on the notifications they receive. The fight against infectious diseases, this research shows, is increasingly a data-driven one.

metrics.pr-title: Study Provides First Real-World Evidence of Covid-19 Contact Tracing App Effectiveness
metrics.pr-abstract: A collaboration of U.K., U.S., and Spanish scientists revealed new insights on the use of digital contact tracing (DCT) to control Covid-19. The team evaluated Spain's Radar Covid DCT application following a four-week experiment in the Canary Islands last summer. Through simulated infections, the authors learned that over 30% of the populace used the mobile phone app, which could detect about 6.3 close-contacts per infected individual, nearly double the national average detected with manual contact tracing. Queen Mary University of London's Lucas Lacasa said, "Overall our results were positive and show that the technology works and if accompanied by appropriate communications campaigns, it should reach the levels of adoption and compliance needed to support other non-pharmaceutical interventions to contain outbreaks." However, he also said the app's privacy-preserving design "severely limits the amount of data that we could collect to accurately assess its performance."

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Quantum Computing Takes a Step Toward Revolutionizing Materials Science**

In a major breakthrough for quantum computing, a new study has demonstrated how quantum computers can be used to simulate and predict the properties of materials with unprecedented accuracy. This research, published in a recent paper, highlights the potential of quantum computing to transform the field of materials science, where understanding atomic and molecular interactions is key to developing new technologies.

Materials science has long relied on classical supercomputers to model the behavior of atoms and electrons in materials. However, these simulations are computationally intensive and often limited by the complexity of quantum systems. Quantum computers, with their ability to represent and manipulate quantum states directly, offer a promising alternative. This paper explores how quantum algorithms can be applied to solve complex material properties, such as magnetic behavior, electronic structure, and chemical reactivity.

The researchers used a quantum algorithm designed to simulate the electronic structure of materials, a fundamental challenge in quantum chemistry. By leveraging the power of quantum parallelism, the algorithm can process vast numbers of quantum states simultaneously, enabling more accurate and efficient predictions of material properties. The study tested the algorithm on a variety of materials, including transition metals and compounds with complex electron interactions, and found that the quantum approach outperformed classical methods in both speed and precision.

Despite these promising results, the paper also acknowledges the current limitations of quantum hardware. The number of qubits required to simulate even moderately complex materials remains high, and error rates in current quantum processors still pose significant challenges. The researchers emphasize that while quantum computing is not yet ready to replace classical simulations entirely, it has the potential to complement them, especially for problems that are intractable for classical computers.

This study represents a crucial step toward the practical application of quantum computing in materials science. By demonstrating the feasibility of quantum simulations for material properties, the research opens the door to new discoveries in energy storage, superconductivity, and advanced electronics. As quantum hardware continues to improve, the integration of quantum computing into materials science could lead to the development of next-generation materials that drive innovation across multiple industries.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

### Meta Info
Title: Quantum Algorithm for High Energy Physics Simulations
### Content
Simulating quantum field theories is a flagship application of quantum computing. However, calculating experimentally relevant high energy scattering amplitudes entirely on a quantum computer is prohibitively difficult. It is well known that such high energy scattering processes can be factored into pieces that can be computed using well established perturbative techniques, and pieces which currently have to be simulated using classical Markov chain algorithms. These classical Markov chain simulation approaches work well to capture many of the salient features, but cannot capture all quantum effects. To exploit quantum resources in the most efficient way, we introduce a new paradigm for quantum algorithms in field theories. This approach uses quantum computers only for those parts of the problem which are not computable using existing techniques. In particular, we develop a polynomial time quantum final state shower that accurately models the effects of intermediate spin states similar to those present in high energy electroweak showers with a global evolution variable. The algorithm is explicitly demonstrated for a simplified quantum field theory on a quantum computer. DOI: 10.1103 / PhysRevLett.126.062001

response:
**Quantum Computing Takes a Leap Forward in High-Energy Physics Simulations**

In a groundbreaking development, researchers have unveiled a novel quantum algorithm designed to tackle one of the most complex challenges in high-energy physics: simulating quantum field theories. The study, published in *Physical Review Letters*, introduces a hybrid approach that leverages quantum computing to fill critical gaps left by classical methods, potentially revolutionizing our understanding of particle interactions.

### The Challenge: Bridging Quantum and Classical Worlds
High-energy physics relies on simulations to model particle collisions and quantum field behaviors. While classical Markov chain algorithms have been the go-to tool for decades, they struggle to capture the full quantum effects of these processes. Quantum computers, with their ability to process superpositions of states, offer a promising solution—but only if used strategically.

### A Smarter Approach: Quantum-Assisted Simulations
The new algorithm takes a hybrid approach, using quantum computing *only* for the parts of the problem that classical methods can’t handle. Specifically, it focuses on modeling intermediate spin states in high-energy electroweak showers—a task that has historically required computationally intensive classical simulations.

By developing a **polynomial-time quantum final state shower**, the researchers demonstrate that quantum computers can accurately simulate these complex quantum effects. The algorithm was successfully tested on a simplified quantum field theory, proving its feasibility.

### Why It Matters
This breakthrough could accelerate discoveries in particle physics, where precise simulations are essential for interpreting experimental data from colliders like the Large Hadron Collider. By combining the strengths of quantum and classical computing, the approach offers a more efficient path forward—one that could unlock new insights into the fundamental laws of the universe.

As quantum hardware continues to improve, this hybrid method may become a cornerstone of high-energy physics research, bridging the gap between theory and experiment in ways never before possible.

**Read the full study:** [DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.126.062001](https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.126.062001)

---
*This report synthesizes the key findings of the research while making them accessible to a broader audience.

metrics.sc-title: Quantum Algorithm for High Energy Physics Simulations
metrics.sc-abstract: Simulating quantum field theories is a flagship application of quantum computing. However, calculating experimentally relevant high energy scattering amplitudes entirely on a quantum computer is prohibitively difficult. It is well known that such high energy scattering processes can be factored into pieces that can be computed using well established perturbative techniques, and pieces which currently have to be simulated using classical Markov chain algorithms. These classical Markov chain simulation approaches work well to capture many of the salient features, but cannot capture all quantum effects. To exploit quantum resources in the most efficient way, we introduce a new paradigm for quantum algorithms in field theories. This approach uses quantum computers only for those parts of the problem which are not computable using existing techniques. In particular, we develop a polynomial time quantum final state shower that accurately models the effects of intermediate spin states similar to those present in high energy electroweak showers with a global evolution variable. The algorithm is explicitly demonstrated for a simplified quantum field theory on a quantum computer. DOI: 10.1103 / PhysRevLett.126.062001
metrics.pr-title: Applying Quantum Computing to a Particle Process
metrics.pr-abstract: Researchers at Lawrence Berkeley National Lab simulated an aspect of particle collisions often neglected in high-energy physics experiments using a quantum computer and an algorithm that accounts for the complexity of parton showers. Said Berkeley Lab's Christian Bauer, "We've essentially shown that you can put a parton shower on a quantum computer with efficient resources, and we've shown there are certain quantum effects that are difficult to describe on a classical computer that you could describe on a quantum computer." The algorithm takes into account particle state, particle emissions history, whether emissions have occurred, and the number of particles generated. Bauer said the quantum computer "computed these histories at the same time, and summed up all of the possible histories at each intermediate stage."

### Meta Info
Title: Gate-dependent spin-orbit coupling in multielectron carbon nanotubes
### Content
Understanding how the orbital motion of electrons is coupled to the spin degree of freedom in nanoscale systems is central for applications in spin-based electronics and quantum computation. Here we demonstrate such spin-orbit coupling in a carbon-nanotube quantum dot in the general multielectron regime and in the presence of finite disorder. Also, we find a systematic dependence of the spin-orbit coupling on the electron occupation of the quantum dot. Such a dependence has not been seen in any other system and follows from the curvature-induced spin-orbit-split Dirac spectrum of the underlying graphene lattice. Our findings suggest that the spin-orbit coupling is a general property of carbon-nanotube quantum dots, which should provide a unique platform for the study of spin-orbit effects and their applications.

Spin-orbit coupling, the phenomenon where an electron's spin interacts with its orbital motion, has long been a subject of intense research in condensed matter physics. However, most studies have focused on bulk materials or two-dimensional systems like graphene. This new work, however, demonstrates that such coupling can be systematically controlled and observed in carbon nanotube quantum dots—nanoscale structures that are essentially rolled-up sheets of graphene.

The researchers attribute this behavior to the curvature-induced spin-orbit-split Dirac spectrum inherent in the graphene lattice from which the nanotubes are formed.

As the field of nanoelectronics continues to evolve, the ability to precisely control spin-orbit coupling in carbon nanotubes could pave the way for more efficient and powerful electronic devices. With further research, these findings may lead to the realization of spin-based technologies that are both faster and more energy-efficient than their traditional counterparts.

## 一、法律依据与基本法律关系分析

本案涉及的主要法律关系包括：

根据《民法典》第719条：

> **“承租人经出租人同意转租的，承租人与出租人之间的租赁合同继续有效，承租人与次承租人之间的租赁合同也有效。出租人可以解除与承租人的租赁合同，但应当在合理期限内通知次承租人。”**

张某与王某之间的租赁合同解除，并不必然导致李某与张某之间的租赁合同无效。李某作为次承租人，其租赁权应受到保护，除非其存在恶意或重大过错。

综上所述，李某作为次承租人，在张某与王某的租赁合同解除前已合法占有房屋，且未参与张某拖欠租金的行为，其租赁权应受到保护。王某虽有权解除与张某的租赁合同，但该解除权并不当然具有对抗李某的效力。李某可以通过主张“善意第三人”地位、租赁权的延续性以及租赁合同的相对性，来抗辩王某的合同解除权，并在合理期限内要求王某通知其搬离，否则其租赁权仍应有效。

## 七、总结

李某在本案中，虽然面临王某解除与张某的租赁合同的风险，但其作为善意次承租人，有权主张其租赁权的延续性，并通过法律手段抗辩王某的解除权。只要其在合理期限内未搬离，且未违反租赁合同约定，其租赁权仍应受到保护。 (Time: 2026-06-01 22:53:41)

## 一、法律依据：《民法典》第720条与第721条

### （一）《民法典》第720条（租赁合同的履行）

### （二）《民法典》第721条（次承租人代付租金的法律效力）

此外，《民法典》第722条也规定：

## 二、法律原理：次承租人代付租金的法律效力

### （一）次承租人代付租金的法律性质

## 三、适用性分析：本案中李某代付租金的可行性

2. **赔礼道歉的性质**  
   赔礼道歉属于一种**人格权保护的救济方式**，具有**精神抚慰**和**恢复名誉**的功能，通常适用于人格权受到侵害的情形，如名誉权、肖像权、隐私权等。

## 二、黎某要求甲公司支付劳动报酬的诉讼请求应如何处理？

### （一）法律依据与分析

1. **劳动报酬请求权的性质**

2. **诉讼请求的性质与法院的审理范围**  
   在本案中，黎某与甲公司之间存在劳动关系，其主张支付劳动报酬属于**劳动争议**，应依法通过**劳动争议仲裁前置程序**解决。根据《中华人民共和国劳动争议调解仲裁法》第5条的规定，劳动争议必须先经劳动争议仲裁委员会仲裁，对仲裁裁决不服的，方可向人民法院提起诉讼。

3. **法院是否应受理黎某的劳动报酬请求？**  
   在本案中，黎某在民事侵权诉讼中提出劳动报酬请求，属于**交叉诉讼**或**反诉**，但法院并未将其纳入审理范围。根据《最高人民法院关于适用〈中华人民共和国民事诉讼法〉的解释》第227条的规定，法院在审理民事案件时，应当审查当事人提出的诉讼请求是否属于本院管辖范围。劳动报酬请求属于劳动争议，应由劳动争议仲裁机构处理，法院不应直接审理。

4. **法院是否应将劳动报酬请求纳入审理？**  
   在本案中，法院未将黎某的劳动报酬请求纳入审理，是基于以下理由：

**一审法院未处理黎某要求甲公司支付劳动报酬的诉讼请求是合法的。**

Title: Imperio: Robust Over-the-Air Adversarial Examples for Automatic Speech Recognition Systems
### Content
Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems can be fooled via targeted adversarial examples, which induce the ASR to produce arbitrary transcriptions in response to altered audio signals. However, state-of-the-art adversarial examples typically have to be fed into the ASR system directly, and are not successful when played in a room. Previously published over-the-air adversarial examples fall into one of three categories: they are either handcrafted examples, they are so conspicuous that human listeners can easily recognize the target transcription once they are alerted to its content, or they require precise information about the room where the attack takes place, and are hence not transferable to other rooms. In this paper, we demonstrate the first algorithm that produces generic adversarial examples against hybrid ASR systems, which remain robust in an over-the-air attack that is not adapted to the specific environment. Hence, no prior knowledge of the room characteristics is required. Instead, we use room impulse responses (RIRs) to compute robust adversarial examples for arbitrary room characteristics and employ the ASR system Kaldi to demonstrate the attack. Further, our algorithm can utilize psychoacoustic methods to hide changes of the original audio signal below the human thresholds of hearing. In practical experiments, we show that the adversarial examples work for varying room setups, and that no direct line-of-sight between speaker and microphone is necessary. As a result, an attacker can create inconspicuous adversarial examples for any target transcription and apply these to arbitrary room setups without any prior knowledge.

### **How to Balance Technical Accuracy with Engaging Storytelling**

1. **Start with the Big Picture**: Frame the research within a broader context (e.g., climate change, energy transition) to make it relevant to a wider audience.

### Meta Info
Title: Snapshot Space-Time Holographic 3D Particle Tracking Velocimetry
### Content
Digital inline holography is an amazingly simple and effective approach for 3D imaging, to which particle tracking velocimetry is of particular interest. Conventional digital holographic particle tracking velocimetry techniques are computationally separated in particle and flow reconstruction, plus the expensive computations. Usually, the particle volumes are recovered first, from which fluid flows are computed. Without iterative reconstructions, This sequential space-time process lacks accuracy. This paper presents a joint optimization framework for digital holographic particle tracking velocimetry: particle volumes and fluid flows are reconstructed jointly in a higher space-time dimension, enabling faster convergence and better reconstruction quality of both fluid flow and particle volumes within a few minutes on modern GPUs. Synthetic and experimental results are presented to show the efficiency of the proposed technique.

response:
**Breaking Barriers in 3D Particle Tracking: How a New Holographic Method Speeds Up Fluid Flow Analysis**

In the world of fluid dynamics, capturing the movement of tiny particles in three dimensions has long been a computational challenge. Traditional methods rely on a slow, two-step process: first reconstructing particle volumes, then calculating fluid flows—a time-consuming and often imprecise approach. But a groundbreaking new technique is turning the tables, offering faster, more accurate results in a fraction of the time.

**The Problem with Conventional Methods**
Digital inline holography has been a game-changer for 3D imaging, but when applied to particle tracking velocimetry (PTV), the process has been anything but efficient. Researchers typically reconstruct particle volumes first, then use those results to infer fluid flows. This sequential approach not only lacks accuracy but also demands heavy computational power, making real-time analysis nearly impossible.

**A Joint Optimization Breakthrough**
Enter *Snapshot Space-Time Holographic 3D Particle Tracking Velocimetry*, a revolutionary framework that reconstructs particle volumes and fluid flows *simultaneously* in a higher-dimensional space-time domain. By eliminating the need for iterative reconstructions, the method achieves faster convergence and superior accuracy—all within minutes on modern GPUs.

**Why It Matters**
The implications are vast. From aerospace engineering to biomedical research, precise 3D particle tracking is crucial for understanding fluid behavior. This new technique could accelerate experiments, reduce costs, and open doors to real-time applications where speed and accuracy are non-negotiable.

**Proven Performance**
The researchers tested their method on both synthetic and real-world data, demonstrating its efficiency and reliability. The results? Faster processing times and sharper reconstructions—proof that this joint optimization approach is a significant leap forward.

As fluid dynamics researchers and engineers race to push the boundaries of what’s possible, this holographic breakthrough could be the key to unlocking new insights in fields where every particle counts.

metrics.pr-title: Harnessing the Particle-Tracking Power of the Algorithm
metrics.pr-abstract: Researchers at Saudi Arabia's King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST) have developed a faster, more accurate three-dimensional (3D) particle-tracking system for monitoring the motion of particles in flow. The researchers developed a numerical algorithm, Holo-Flow, that identifies the location and motion of particles in parallel, and cross-feeds the information in each step. KAUST's Congli Wang said, "Inline holography requires fewer components, has a much simpler setup, can be easily used with microscopes and offers a higher spatial resolution, but is harder to solve numerically. We have shown that we can achieve the same or even better performance than conventional methods by using sophisticated software algorithms."

### Meta Info
Title: PrinTracker: Fingerprinting 3D Printers using Commodity Scanners
### Content
As 3D printing technology begins to outpace traditional manufacturing, malicious users increasingly have sought to leverage this widely accessible platform to produce unlawful tools for criminal activities. Therefore, it is of paramount importance to identify the origin of unlawful 3D printed products using digital forensics. Traditional countermeasures, including information embedding or watermarking, rely on supervised manufacturing process and are impractical for identifying the origin of 3D printed tools in criminal applications. We argue that 3D printers possess unique fingerprints, which arise from hardware imperfections during the manufacturing process, causing discrepancies in the line formation of printed physical objects. These variations appear repeatedly and result in unique textures that can serve as a viable fingerprint on associated 3D printed products. To address the challenge of traditional forensics in identifying unlawful 3D printed products, we present PrinTracker, the 3D printer identification system, which can precisely trace the physical object to its source 3D printer based on its fingerprint. Results indicate that PrinTracker provides a high accuracy using 14 different 3D printers. Under unfavorable conditions (e.g. restricted sample area, location and process), the PrinTracker can still achieve an acceptable accuracy of 92%. Furthermore, we examine the effectiveness, robustness, reliability and vulnerability of the PrinTracker in multiple real-world scenarios.
assistant: **Breaking the Code: How 3D Printers Leave Digital Fingerprints**

*By [Your Name], Tech Correspondent*

In an era where 3D printing has become as accessible as a home printer, a new frontier of digital forensics is emerging. A groundbreaking study, titled *PrinTracker: Fingerprinting 3D Printers using Commodity Scanners*, has unveiled a method to trace the origin of 3D-printed objects with remarkable precision—without requiring any changes to the manufacturing process.

As 3D printing technology continues to evolve, it has also become a tool for illicit activities. From counterfeit parts to weapons, the ease of access to 3D printers has raised serious concerns about security and accountability. Traditional methods of tracking the source of 3D-printed items—such as embedding watermarks or using digital signatures—have proven impractical, especially in criminal contexts where such measures are often bypassed or not used at all.

Enter *PrinTracker*, a novel system that leverages the inherent imperfections of 3D printers to identify their origin. The research team behind PrinTracker argues that every 3D printer, no matter how advanced, has unique hardware characteristics that manifest as subtle variations in the printed objects. These variations, caused by minute imperfections in the printer’s mechanics and materials, create distinct textures and patterns—essentially a "fingerprint" that can be traced back to the original machine.

Using commodity scanners, PrinTracker captures these fingerprints and maps them to specific 3D printers. The system was tested across 14 different printers and demonstrated impressive accuracy, even under challenging conditions such as limited sample areas or restricted printing environments. In such scenarios, PrinTracker still achieved an accuracy rate of 92%, proving its robustness in real-world applications.

The implications of this technology are profound. Law enforcement agencies and cybersecurity experts could use PrinTracker to trace the source of illegal 3D-printed items, potentially dismantling illicit supply chains. Moreover, the system’s ability to work without altering the printing process makes it a powerful tool for digital forensics in an age where 3D printing is becoming increasingly ubiquitous.

While the study highlights the effectiveness of PrinTracker, it also acknowledges the need for further research into its vulnerabilities and potential countermeasures. As 3D printing continues to reshape manufacturing and security landscapes, tools like PrinTracker may become essential in maintaining accountability and preventing misuse.

In the words of the researchers, “Every 3D printer has a unique signature. With PrinTracker, we can now read it.”
