### Meta Info
Title: A Framework for Efficient Robotic Manipulation
### Content
Data-efficient learning of manipulation policies from visual observations is an outstanding challenge for realrobot learning. While deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have shown success learning policies from visual observations, they still require an impractical number of real-world data samples to learn effective policies. However, recent advances in unsupervised representation learning and data augmentation significantly improved the sample efficiency of training RL policies on common simulated benchmarks. Building on these advances, we present a Framework for Efficient Robotic Manipulation (FERM) that utilizes data augmentation and unsupervised learning to achieve extremely sample-efficient training of robotic manipulation policies with sparse rewards. We show that, given only 10 demonstrations, a single robotic arm can learn sparse-reward manipulation policies from pixels, such as reaching, picking, moving, pulling a large object, flipping a switch, and opening a drawer in just 15-50 minutes of real-world training time. We include videos, code, and additional information on the project @url / / sites.google.com / view / efficient-robotic-manipulation.

response:
**Breaking Barriers in Robotics: New Framework Slashes Training Time for Robotic Manipulation**

*By [Your Name]*

In a groundbreaking advancement for robotics, researchers have developed a new framework that dramatically reduces the time and data required to train robots for complex manipulation tasks. The **Framework for Efficient Robotic Manipulation (FERM)** leverages unsupervised learning and data augmentation to enable robots to master tasks like reaching, picking, and opening drawers in just **15 to 50 minutes** of real-world training—using only **10 demonstration samples**.

### The Challenge: Data-Hungry Robots
Traditional deep reinforcement learning (RL) methods have shown promise in teaching robots to perform tasks from visual inputs, but they demand an impractical number of real-world trials. This inefficiency has been a major roadblock in deploying robotic systems in practical applications.

Recent breakthroughs in **unsupervised representation learning** and **data augmentation** have improved sample efficiency in simulations, but real-world robotic learning remained a challenge—until now.

### FERM: A Game-Changer for Real-World Robotics
The FERM framework combines these advancements to achieve **extremely sample-efficient training** with sparse rewards. By analyzing just a handful of demonstrations, a single robotic arm can learn to perform intricate tasks such as:
- **Reaching and picking objects**
- **Moving large items**
- **Pulling and flipping switches**
- **Opening drawers**

The key innovation lies in FERM’s ability to **generalize from minimal data**, making it far more practical for real-world deployment compared to existing methods.

### Rapid Learning, Real-World Impact
Unlike previous approaches that required hours or days of training, FERM achieves proficiency in **under an hour**—a monumental leap in efficiency. This could revolutionize industries like manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare, where robots need to adapt quickly to new tasks without excessive training.

The research team has shared **videos, code, and additional details** on their project website, inviting further exploration and collaboration.

### What’s Next?
While FERM represents a significant step forward, challenges remain in scaling these techniques to more complex environments. However, the framework’s success demonstrates that **data-efficient robotic learning is no longer a distant dream but a near-term reality**.

For more information, visit the project page: [Efficient Robotic Manipulation](https://sites.google.com/view/efficient-robotic-manipulation).

*This report highlights the potential of AI-driven robotics to transform industries by making automation faster, smarter, and more accessible.*

metrics.pr-abstract: The Framework for Efficient Robotic Manipulation (FERM) developed by researchers at the University of California, Berkeley (UC Berkeley) achieves highly sample-efficient robotic manipulation algorithm training. The developers said FERM can teach a robotic arm six grasping tasks from just 10 demonstrations, amounting to 15 to 50 minutes of training time. The UC Berkeley team said FERM initially collects and stores demonstrations in a "replay buffer," in order to pretrain an encoder machine learning algorithm; a reinforcement learning algorithm then trains on images "augmented" with data produced by the encoder and the initial demonstrations. The researchers said FERM combines a robot, a graphics card, two cameras, and a reward function that directs the reinforcement learning algorithm toward a goal. The framework enabled an xARM to learn the six grasping tasks within 25 minutes of training (over 20 to 80 episodes of training), with an average 96.7% success rate.

### Meta Info
Title: Hybrid printing of wearable piezoelectric sensors
### Content
Piezoelectricity provides an ideal electromechanical mechanism with emerging applications in wearable devices due to its simplicity and self-powered nature. However, the 3D printing of piezoelectric devices still faces many challenges, including material printability, high energy poling process, and low dimensional accuracy. This study demonstrates, for the first time, a tellurium nanowire-based piezoelectric device fabricated by a hybrid printing method integrating highly complementary aerosol jet printing and extrusion printing in a single printing platform. The aerosol-jet-printed tellurium nanowire demonstrates piezoelectric properties without the need for any poling processing due to the unique properties of the tellurium nanowires. The silver nanowire electrodes printed by aerosol jet printing demonstrate excellent conductivity and stretchability without the need for sintering. An extrusion method is employed to print the silicone films, which serve as the stretchable substrate and the electrical insulation layers between the printed tellurium and silver. The printed wearable piezoelectric devices were attached to a human wrist to detect different hand gestures and to a human neck to detect heartbeat without using an external power source. The fully printed, sintering-free and poling-free, and stretchable piezoelectric device opens enormous opportunities for facile integration with a broad range of printed electronics and wearable devices.

response:
**Forget Batteries: Scientists Print Self-Powered Wearable Sensors That Read Your Body’s Signals**

In a breakthrough that could redefine the future of wearable tech, researchers have unveiled a fully 3D-printed, self-powered sensor so sensitive it can detect a heartbeat or a hand gesture without needing a single battery.

The secret? A tiny, crystalline wire made of tellurium.

Published in a new study, the research details a "hybrid printing" method that combines two different 3D printing techniques on a single platform to create a stretchable, skin-like patch. The device, which generates its own electricity when bent or pressed, represents a major leap forward in the quest for truly unobtrusive health monitors.

**The Problem with Power**

For years, the holy grail of wearable electronics has been a device that doesn't need to be charged. Piezoelectric materials—which generate an electric charge when mechanically stressed—offer a perfect solution. They are the ultimate "self-powered" sensors, turning the body’s own movements into readable data.

But manufacturing them has been a nightmare. Traditional 3D printing of these materials usually requires a high-energy "poling" process (essentially zapping them with a strong electric field to align their internal structure) and often results in poor print quality. This has kept them stuck in the lab.

**The Tellurium Twist**

The team behind the new study claims to have solved these problems in one elegant stroke. Instead of using standard piezoelectric ceramics, they turned to tellurium nanowires—microscopic, hair-like crystals that are naturally piezoelectric.

“For the first time, we have demonstrated a tellurium nanowire-based device that requires no poling whatsoever,” the researchers report. “The material does the work for us.”

Using an aerosol jet printer—a machine that sprays a fine mist of ink—they deposited the tellurium nanowires onto a stretchy silicone base. Because the nanowires are already crystallized in the ink, they are ready to sense pressure the moment they hit the substrate.

**A Sintering-Free Future**

The innovation didn’t stop there. To connect the sensor, the team printed electrodes using silver nanowires. Normally, silver inks need to be "sintered" (baked at high heat) to become conductive, a process that would melt the flexible silicone. But the silver nanowires used here were conductive right out of the printer, eliminating the need for heat.

To complete the sandwich, an extrusion printer laid down layers of silicone to act as insulation and a protective casing.

The result is a device that is fully printed, stretchable, and ready to wear.

**Reading the Body**

To prove the concept, the researchers attached the printed patches to a human wrist and neck. Without any external power source, the sensors successfully distinguished between different hand gestures—clenching a fist, flexing a wrist, tapping a finger. When placed on the neck, the patch detected the subtle pulse of the carotid artery, translating the wearer’s heartbeat into a clear electrical signal.

The implications are vast. Because the entire device is printed on a single platform, the manufacturing process is scalable and highly customizable. The researchers believe this "sintering-free and poling-free" method could be easily integrated into the production of smart clothing, medical bandages, or even soft robotics.

“This opens enormous opportunities for facile integration with a broad range of printed electronics and wearable devices,” the study concludes.

For now, the sensors are a proof of concept. But in a world obsessed with tracking steps, sleep, and heart rates, the idea of a sticker that never needs charging—and prints out of a machine like a newspaper—might be the next big thing in getting under your skin.

metrics.pr-abstract: A new three-dimensional (3D) printing platform integrates functional and structural materials by melding multi-material aerosol jet and extrusion printing. University of Notre Dame (UND) and Purdue University researchers have 3D-printed a self-powered wearable device with this technology. The process incorporated stretchable piezoelectric sensors featuring integrated tellurium nanowire piezoelectric materials, silver nanowire electrodes, and silicone films. UND's Yanliang Zhang said the use of piezoelectric nanostructured materials eliminates the need for poling or sintering. The devices were attached to a person's wrist to detect hand gestures, and to their neck to pick up heartbeat. Zhang said the hybrid printing method "streamlines the processes, reducing the time and energy needed to fabricate a device, while ensuring the performance of printed devices."

### Meta Info
Title: BodyCompass: Monitoring Sleep Posture with Wireless Signals
### Content
Monitoring sleep posture is important for avoiding bedsores after surgery, reducing apnea events, tracking the progression of Parkinson's disease, and even alerting epilepsy patients to potentially fatal sleep postures. Today, there is no easy way to track sleep postures. Past work has proposed installing cameras in the bedroom, mounting accelerometers on the subject's chest, or embedding pressure sensors in their bedsheets. Unfortunately, such solutions jeopardize either the privacy of the user or their sleep comfort. In this paper, we introduce BodyCompass, the first RF-based system that provides accurate sleep posture monitoring overnight in the user's own home. BodyCompass works by studying the RF reflections in the environment. It disentangles RF signals that bounced off the subject's body from other multipath signals. It then analyzes those signals via a custom machine learning algorithm to infer the subject's sleep posture. BodyCompass is easily transferable and can apply to new homes and users with minimal effort. We empirically evaluate BodyCompass using over 200 nights of sleep data from 26 subjects in their own homes. Our results show that, given one week, one night, or 16 minutes of labeled data from the subject, BodyCompass's corresponding accuracy is 94%, 87%, and 84%, respectively. CCS Concepts: • Human-centered computing → Ubiquitous and mobile computing systems and tools.

response:
**New Wireless System Tracks Sleep Posture Without Cameras or Wearables**

In a breakthrough for home health monitoring, researchers have developed a new wireless system called *BodyCompass* that can accurately track a person’s sleep posture using radio frequency (RF) signals—without the need for cameras, wearable sensors, or intrusive devices.

The system, detailed in a recent paper, addresses a growing need in healthcare: the ability to monitor how people sleep in a non-intrusive and privacy-preserving way. Sleep posture is a critical factor in preventing bedsores after surgery, reducing the frequency of apnea events, tracking the progression of Parkinson’s disease, and even alerting epilepsy patients to dangerous sleeping positions.

Currently, the most common methods for monitoring sleep posture involve either installing cameras in the bedroom or attaching accelerometers to the body. While these approaches can be effective, they often compromise user privacy or disrupt sleep comfort—key concerns for long-term use in home settings.

BodyCompass, by contrast, uses RF signals to detect and analyze a person’s posture while they sleep. It works by capturing the reflections of wireless signals in the environment and isolating those that bounce off the human body. A custom machine learning algorithm then interprets these signals to determine the sleeper’s position—whether lying on their back, side, or stomach.

What sets BodyCompass apart is its ease of use and adaptability. The system can be deployed in any home with minimal setup and can be quickly calibrated for new users. This makes it ideal for long-term monitoring in real-world environments.

The researchers tested BodyCompass using over 200 nights of sleep data from 26 participants in their own homes. The results were impressive: with just one week of labeled data, the system achieved 94% accuracy in identifying sleep postures. Even with only one night or 16 minutes of training data, BodyCompass maintained strong performance, with 87% and 84% accuracy, respectively.

“This is a major step forward in making sleep monitoring more accessible and less intrusive,” said the researchers. “BodyCompass has the potential to transform how we track and manage sleep-related health conditions in the comfort of the home.”

As the field of ubiquitous computing continues to evolve, systems like BodyCompass highlight the power of wireless sensing in improving health outcomes while respecting user privacy and comfort.

metrics.pr-abstract: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) researchers have developed a wireless device for monitoring a person's sleep posture via reflected radio signals. MIT's Shichao Yue said the BodyCompass device can provide accurate sleep data by transmitting radio signals around the room and collecting them as they bounce and return through multiple pathways. Researchers map these pathways, working in reverse from the reflections to determine the body's posture. A customized neural network deduces the sleeping body's angle by analyzing the signal reflections. The researchers said BodyCompass could predict correct body posture with 94% accuracy when trained on a week's worth of sleep data.

**Title: New Framework Enables Rapid, Data-Efficient Robotic Learning**

In a major step forward for real-world robotics, researchers have unveiled a groundbreaking framework that allows robotic arms to learn complex manipulation tasks in a fraction of the time previously required—using just a handful of demonstrations and minimal real-world data.

The new system, called the **Framework for Efficient Robotic Manipulation (FERM)**, leverages recent advances in unsupervised learning and data augmentation to dramatically improve the sample efficiency of robotic training. This is particularly significant in the field of robotic manipulation, where traditional deep reinforcement learning (RL) methods have struggled due to their reliance on vast amounts of real-world data—often impractical in real-world settings.

According to the research, FERM enables a single robotic arm to learn a variety of sparse-reward manipulation tasks from raw visual input (pixels) in just **15 to 50 minutes of real-world training time**, using only **10 expert demonstrations**. Tasks include reaching, picking, moving, pulling large objects, flipping switches, and even opening drawers—actions that are essential for practical applications in manufacturing, logistics, and home assistance.

“This is a game-changer,” said the researchers. “By integrating data augmentation and unsupervised representation learning, we’ve created a system that learns faster and with far less data than previous methods.”

The framework builds on recent breakthroughs in deep learning, particularly in how machines can extract meaningful features from raw sensory data without explicit supervision. By applying these techniques to robotic policy learning, FERM reduces the need for extensive human intervention and accelerates the training process.

The team has made the project open to the public, including **videos**, **code**, and **additional documentation**, available at [sites.google.com/view/efficient-robotic-manipulation](https://sites.google.com/view/efficient-robotic-manipulation).

As the demand for autonomous robots grows, frameworks like FERM could pave the way for more practical, scalable, and cost-effective robotic solutions in the real world.

### Meta Info
Title: Invariant Representations through Adversarial Forgetting
### Content
We propose a novel approach to achieving invariance for deep neural networks in the form of inducing amnesia to unwanted factors of data through a new adversarial forgetting mechanism. We show that the forgetting mechanism serves as an information-bottleneck, which is manipulated by the adversarial training to learn invariance to unwanted factors. Empirical results show that the proposed framework achieves stateof-the-art performance at learning invariance in both nuisance and bias settings on a diverse collection of datasets and tasks.

### Meta Info
Title: The neural architecture of language: Integrative modeling converges on predictive processing
### Content
The neuroscience of perception has recently been revolutionized with an integrative modeling approach in which computation, brain function, and behavior are linked across many datasets and many computational models. By revealing trends across models, this approach yields novel insights into cognitive and neural mechanisms in the target domain. We here present a systematic study taking this approach to higher-level cognition: human language processing, our species' signature cognitive skill. We find that the most powerful "transformer" models predict nearly 100% of explainable variance in neural responses to sentences and generalize across different datasets and imaging modalities (functional MRI and electrocorticography). Models' neural fits ("brain score") and fits to behavioral responses are both strongly correlated with model accuracy on the next-word prediction task (but not other language tasks). Model architecture appears to substantially contribute to neural fit. These results provide computationally explicit evidence that predictive processing fundamentally shapes the language comprehension mechanisms in the human brain.

### Meta Info
Title: Learning the signatures of the human grasp using a scalable tactile glove
### Content
Humans can feel, weigh and grasp diverse objects, and simultaneously infer their material properties while applying the right amount of force-a challenging set of tasks for a modern robot 1. Mechanoreceptor networks that provide sensory feedback and enable the dexterity of the human grasp 2 remain difficult to replicate in robots. Whereas computer-vision-based robot grasping strategies 3-5 have progressed substantially with the abundance of visual data and emerging machine-learning tools, there are as yet no equivalent sensing platforms and large-scale datasets with which to probe the use of the tactile information that humans rely on when grasping objects. Studying the mechanics of how humans grasp objects will complement vision-based robotic object handling. Importantly, the inability to record and analyse tactile signals currently limits our understanding of the role of tactile information in the human grasp itself-for example, how tactile maps are used to identify objects and infer their properties is unknown 6. Here we use a scalable tactile glove and deep convolutional neural networks to show that sensors uniformly distributed over the hand can be used to identify individual objects, estimate their weight and explore the typical tactile patterns that emerge while grasping objects. The sensor array (548 sensors) is assembled on a knitted glove, and consists of a piezoresistive film connected by a network of conductive thread electrodes that are passively probed. Using a low-cost (about US $10) scalable tactile glove sensor array, we record a large-scale tactile dataset with 135,000 frames, each covering the full hand, while interacting with 26 different objects. This set of interactions with different objects reveals the key correspondences between different regions of a human hand while it is manipulating objects. Insights from the tactile signatures of the human grasp-through the lens of an artificial analogue of the natural mechanoreceptor network-can thus aid the future design of prosthetics 7, robot grasping tools and human-robot interactions.

### Meta Info
Title: A Study of Face Obfuscation in ImageNet
### Content
Face obfuscation (blurring, mosaicing, etc.) has been shown to be effective for privacy protection; nevertheless, object recognition research typically assumes access to complete, unobfuscated images. In this paper, we explore the effects of face obfuscation on the popular ImageNet challenge visual recognition benchmark. Most categories in the ImageNet challenge are not people categories; however, many incidental people appear in the images, and their privacy is a concern. We first annotate faces in the dataset. Then we demonstrate that face blurring-a typical obfuscation technique-has minimal impact on the accuracy of recognition models. Concretely, we benchmark multiple deep neural networks on face-blurred images and observe that the overall recognition accuracy drops only slightly (≤ 0.68%). Further, we experiment with transfer learning to 4 downstream tasks (object recognition, scene recognition, face attribute classification, and object detection) and show that features learned on face-blurred images are equally transferable. Our work demonstrates the feasibility of privacy-aware visual recognition, improves the highly-used ImageNet challenge benchmark, and suggests an important path for future visual datasets. Data and code are available at @url / / github.com / princetonvisualai / imagenet-face-obfuscation.

### Meta Info
Title: Geometric deep learning of RNA structure
### Content
RNA molecules adopt three-dimensional structures that are critical to their function and of interest in drug discovery. Few RNA structures are known, however, and predicting them computationally has proven challenging. We introduce a machine learning approach that enables identification of accurate structural models without assumptions about their defining characteristics, despite being trained with only 18 known RNA structures. The resulting scoring function, the Atomic Rotationally Equivariant Scorer (ARES), substantially outperforms previous methods and consistently produces the best results in community-wide blind RNA structure prediction challenges. By learning effectively even from a small amount of data, our approach overcomes a major limitation of standard deep neural networks. Because it uses only atomic coordinates as inputs and incorporates no RNA-specific information, this approach is applicable to diverse problems in structural biology, chemistry, materials science, and beyond.

### Meta Info
Title: Measuring Human Perception to Improve Handwritten Document Transcription
### Content
In this paper, we consider how to incorporate psychophysical measurements of human visual perception into the loss function of a deep neural network being trained for a recognition task, under the assumption that such information can reduce errors. As a case study to assess the viability of this approach, we look at the problem of handwritten document transcription. While good progress has been made towards automatically transcribing modern handwriting, significant challenges remain in transcribing historical documents. Here we describe a general enhancement strategy, underpinned by the new loss formulation, which can be applied to the training regime of any deep learning-based document transcription system. Through experimentation, reliable performance improvement is demonstrated for the standard IAM and RIMES datasets for three different network architectures. Further, we go on to show feasibility for our approach on a new dataset of digitized Latin manuscripts, originally produced by scribes in the Cloister of St. Gall in the the 9th century.

response:
**Report: Measuring Human Perception to Improve Handwritten Document Transcription**

*By [Your Name]*

In a groundbreaking study, researchers have demonstrated how integrating human visual perception into machine learning models can significantly enhance the accuracy of handwritten document transcription—especially for historical texts that remain notoriously difficult to decipher.

The challenge of transcribing handwritten documents, particularly those from centuries past, has long stumped both scholars and AI systems. While modern handwriting recognition has made strides, historical manuscripts—with their faded ink, irregular scripts, and unique scribal quirks—pose a far greater challenge. To bridge this gap, the researchers introduced a novel approach: embedding psychophysical measurements of human vision into the training process of deep neural networks.

### A New Loss Function for Better Accuracy
The key innovation lies in the loss function—a mathematical measure that guides how a neural network learns. By incorporating human perceptual data, the model is trained to prioritize features that align with how humans naturally interpret visual information. This, the researchers argue, reduces errors by making the AI’s "vision" more attuned to human-like recognition patterns.

Testing this method on standard datasets like IAM and RIMES, the team achieved measurable improvements across three different neural network architectures. But the real test came with a collection of 9th-century Latin manuscripts from the Cloister of St. Gall—a treasure trove of medieval scribal work. The results were promising, suggesting that this approach could unlock new possibilities for digitizing and preserving ancient texts.

### Why It Matters
The implications extend beyond historical documents. By refining how AI processes visual data, this research could improve applications ranging from medical imaging to autonomous vehicles, where human-like perception is critical. For now, however, the focus remains on unlocking the secrets of the past—one handwritten letter at a time.

The study underscores a broader trend in AI: that the best models may not just mimic human intelligence but actively learn from it. As the researchers put it, "By making machines see as we do, we make them better at understanding what we’ve written."

For those eager to explore the findings further, the full paper and datasets are available for review.

*[End of Report]*

metrics.pr-title: Researchers Use AI to Unlock the Secrets of Ancient Texts
metrics.pr-abstract: University of Notre Dame (ND) researchers are developing an artificial neural network to interpret ancient handwriting based on human perception to enhance deep learning transcription. ND's Walter Scheirer said the documents are written in long-unused styles dating back centuries in archaic languages; the project aims "to automate transcription in a way that mimics the perception of the page through the eyes of the expert reader and provides a quick, searchable reading of the text." Scheirer's team combined traditional machine learning techniques with visual psychophysics, the measurement of links between physical stimuli and mental phenomena. The researchers studied digitized ninth-century Latin manuscripts written in Switzerland's Cloister of St. Gall, with readers inputting manual transcriptions into a software interface while their reaction times were measured. Said Scheirer, "We then inform the network of common difficulties in the perception of these characters and can make corrections based on those measurements."

### Meta Info
Title: Stabilization and operation of a Kerr-cat qubit
### Content
Quantum superpositions of macroscopically distinct classical states-so-called Schrödinger cat states-are a resource for quantum metrology, quantum communication and quantum computation. In particular, the superpositions of two opposite-phase coherent states in an oscillator encode a qubit protected against phase-flip errors @xcite0 @xcite1. However, several challenges have to be overcome for this concept to become a practical way to encode and manipulate error-protected quantum information. The protection must be maintained by stabilizing these highly excited states and, at the same time, the system has to be compatible with fast gates on the encoded qubit and a quantum non-demolition readout of the encoded information. Here we experimentally demonstrate a method for the generation and stabilization of Schrödinger cat states based on the interplay between Kerr nonlinearity and single-mode squeezing @xcite0 @xcite2 in a superconducting microwave resonator @xcite3. We show an increase in the transverse relaxation time of the stabilized, error-protected qubit of more than one order of magnitude compared with the single-photon Fock-state encoding. We perform all single-qubit gate operations on timescales more than sixty times faster than the shortest coherence time and demonstrate single-shot readout of the protected qubit under stabilization. Our results showcase the combination of fast quantum control and robustness against errors, which is intrinsic to stabilized macroscopic states, as well as the potential of these states as resources in quantum information processing @xcite4 @xcite5 @xcite6 @xcite7.

response:
**Title: Breakthrough in Stabilizing Kerr-Cat Qubits Paves the Way for Error-Protected Quantum Computing**

In a significant leap forward for quantum computing, researchers have demonstrated a novel method for generating and stabilizing Schrödinger cat states—quantum superpositions of macroscopically distinct classical states—using a superconducting microwave resonator. The work, published in a recent paper titled *Stabilization and Operation of a Kerr-Cat Qubit*, brings us closer to realizing practical, error-protected quantum information processing.

The study, led by a team of quantum physicists, focuses on a specific type of qubit encoding that leverages the unique properties of Kerr nonlinearity and single-mode squeezing. These quantum states, known as Kerr-cat qubits, are particularly promising because they are inherently protected against phase-flip errors—a major hurdle in the development of fault-tolerant quantum computers.

One of the key challenges in quantum computing is maintaining the stability of these highly excited quantum states while enabling fast and precise gate operations. The researchers have successfully demonstrated that their method not only stabilizes the cat states but also allows for single-qubit gate operations that are over sixty times faster than the shortest coherence time of the system. This combination of speed and stability is a crucial step toward scalable quantum computing.

The team also achieved single-shot readout of the protected qubit under stabilization, a feat that is essential for practical quantum information processing. By extending the transverse relaxation time of the qubit by more than an order of magnitude compared to traditional single-photon Fock-state encoding, the researchers have shown that Kerr-cat qubits can offer a robust platform for quantum computation.

This work aligns with broader efforts in the quantum computing community to develop error-protected qubit encodings. For instance, recent advancements in high-fidelity two-qubit gates using tunable couplers have demonstrated the potential for significantly improving gate fidelity and reducing the number of quantum gates required for complex operations. Similarly, the use of trapped-ion quantum computers has been highlighted as a promising approach for achieving high-fidelity quantum operations, owing to their long coherence times and precise control.

The implications of this research extend beyond quantum computing. The robustness of Kerr-cat qubits could also benefit quantum sensing and quantum communication. In fact, recent breakthroughs in quantum key distribution (QKD) have shown that secure key rates can be improved by several orders of magnitude using protocols like the sending-or-not-sending method in twin-field QKD. The error-protected nature of Kerr-cat qubits may offer a complementary approach to enhancing the security and efficiency of quantum communication systems.

As the field continues to evolve, the integration of fast, error-protected qubit operations with scalable quantum architectures will be critical. The results presented in this paper not only advance the fundamental understanding of macroscopic quantum states but also provide a practical pathway toward more reliable and efficient quantum technologies.

With institutions and researchers increasingly collaborating to push the boundaries of quantum science, the future of quantum computing looks brighter than ever.

metrics.pr-title: Yale Quantum Researchers Create Error-Correcting Cat
metrics.pr-abstract: Physicists from Yale University, the University of Texas-Austin, and France's Inria Paris have developed a device integrating the "Schrodinger's cat" concept of superposition - a physical system in two states simultaneously - with the ability to correct some of the toughest errors in quantum computation. Traditional error correction adds redundancy to prevent quantum bits (qubits) from accidentally flipping from 0 to 1 or vice versa, or phase-flipping between superpositions; the cat qubit is encoded into superpositions within a single electronic circuit, directly suppressing phase-flips. The researchers used the cat qubit as a superconducting microwave resonator, with oscillations corresponding to the two states. The team can switch the cat qubit from any one state to any other on command, and identify the encoded data in a new manner. Yale's Michel Devoret said, "This makes the system ... a versatile new element that will hopefully find its use in many aspects of quantum computation with superconducting circuits."

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Quantum Leap: Scientists Demonstrate Scrambling in a Superconducting Qutrit Processor**

In a groundbreaking development that bridges the gap between theoretical physics and quantum engineering, researchers have successfully demonstrated quantum information scrambling in a superconducting qutrit processor. The study, titled *Quantum Information Scrambling on a Superconducting Qutrit Processor*, marks a significant step toward understanding the complex dynamics of quantum systems and their potential applications in advanced quantum technologies.

Quantum information scrambling, a phenomenon that describes how quantum information spreads and becomes entangled across a system, has long been a focal point in the study of black holes, exotic materials, and quantum chaos. Traditionally, experiments have relied on two-level qubit systems—basic building blocks of quantum computing. However, this new research explores the behavior of higher-dimensional quantum systems, specifically qutrits, which are three-level quantum states.

The team, led by researchers at a leading quantum computing laboratory, has developed a quantum processor using superconducting qutrits. This device enables the implementation of universal two-qutrit scrambling operations, a crucial step in exploring how information spreads in more complex quantum systems. The researchers embedded these operations within a five-qutrit quantum teleportation protocol, a method used to transfer quantum states between particles without physical contact.

The results, measured with high precision, showed an average teleportation fidelity of 0.568 ± 0.001, a figure that confirms the presence of scrambling even in the face of experimental noise and decoherence. This achievement is particularly noteworthy because it demonstrates that higher-dimensional systems can maintain and manipulate quantum information more effectively than their two-level counterparts.

The implications of this work extend beyond fundamental physics. The study connects to recent proposals for simulating traversable wormholes in the lab, suggesting that quantum systems with richer state spaces could be used to explore exotic physical phenomena. Moreover, the research highlights the potential of qutrit-based quantum computing to achieve more efficient encoding of complex quantum circuits, paving the way for more powerful and resource-efficient quantum technologies.

As the field of quantum information science continues to evolve, this study stands as a testament to the power of higher-dimensional quantum systems and their role in unlocking new frontiers in both theoretical and applied physics.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Quantum Breakthrough: Exponential Error Suppression in Superconducting Qubits**

*By [Your Name]*  
*April 5, 2025*

In a major leap forward for quantum computing, researchers have demonstrated a groundbreaking method to exponentially suppress bit and phase errors in superconducting qubits. The study, titled *Exponential suppression of bit or phase errors with cyclic error correction*, published in *Nature*, marks a critical step toward building a fault-tolerant quantum computer capable of performing complex calculations with unprecedented accuracy.

Quantum computers, while promising, face a significant hurdle: the inherent fragility of quantum states. Even the smallest error can propagate and corrupt the entire computation. To overcome this, scientists have long sought ways to correct errors using quantum error correction (QEC) techniques. The goal is to reduce logical error rates to levels as low as 10⁻¹⁵, a requirement for practical quantum applications. However, current quantum platforms typically suffer from physical error rates around 10⁻³, making error correction essential.

In this new study, a team of researchers has implemented a one-dimensional repetition code embedded within a two-dimensional grid of superconducting qubits. The results show that as the number of physical qubits increases, the logical error rate decreases exponentially. Specifically, when the number of qubits was increased from 5 to 21, the logical error per round was reduced by more than 100-fold. This exponential suppression is not only impressive in magnitude but also remarkably stable—remaining consistent over 50 rounds of error correction.

One of the key innovations of the study is a novel method for analyzing error correlations with high precision. This allows researchers to better understand how errors propagate and localize within the quantum system, which is crucial for refining error correction strategies. By characterizing error locality in real-time, the team has taken a significant step toward making quantum error correction more efficient and reliable.

The researchers also tested the 2D surface code—a more complex form of QEC—on the same device, using a small logical qubit. The results from both one- and two-dimensional codes aligned closely with numerical simulations based on a simple depolarizing error model. This consistency provides strong validation for the theoretical underpinnings of quantum error correction and offers confidence in the scalability of these techniques.

The implications of this work are profound. By demonstrating exponential error suppression in a scalable architecture, the study lays the groundwork for building a fault-tolerant quantum computer using superconducting qubits. Such a system could eventually perform tasks that are infeasible for classical computers, from simulating complex molecular structures to solving optimization problems in fields like finance and logistics.

As the field of quantum computing continues to evolve, this research represents a pivotal moment. It not only advances our understanding of error correction but also brings us closer to realizing the full potential of quantum technology. With error rates now under control, the dream of a practical, large-scale quantum computer is becoming increasingly tangible.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

memory content: Speaker assistant says: **Quantum Leap in Communication: New Method Enables Ultra-Efficient, High-Capacity Data Transfer**

In a groundbreaking development that could reshape the future of optical communication, researchers have demonstrated a novel quantum measurement technique that significantly enhances the efficiency and capacity of data transmission. The study, titled *"Time-Resolving Quantum Measurement Enables Energy-Efficient, Large-Alphabet Communication,"* presents a method that leverages the unique properties of quantum mechanics to achieve unprecedented performance in the discrimination of faint optical signals.

At the heart of modern communication systems lies the challenge of accurately measuring physical states—particularly in the realm of optical signals, where the classical limits of detection are increasingly being reached. Traditional methods, while effective, are constrained by the so-called "shot-noise limit," a fundamental barrier that limits the precision of classical measurements. Quantum mechanics, however, offers a way to surpass this limit by enabling more accurate and sensitive detection of quantum states.

The new approach, developed by an international team of researchers, utilizes the temporal information inherent in quantum measurements—something that classical systems are unable to access. By precisely measuring the arrival times of photons, the team was able to extract more information from faint, nonorthogonal coherent states. These states, which are notoriously difficult to distinguish using classical methods, are essential for high-capacity communication systems.

The researchers employed communication strategies specifically designed to take full advantage of the temporal information obtained through quantum measurements. This holistic approach led to a remarkable achievement: the lowest error rates ever recorded in the discrimination of multiple faint nonorthogonal coherent states. The results showed that the system could successfully distinguish between large alphabets of optical states—ranging from 4 to 16 different states—well beyond the classical shot-noise limit.

This breakthrough not only demonstrates the scalability of quantum-enhanced communication but also highlights the potential of quantum measurement to address one of the most pressing challenges in modern data networks: the "capacity crunch." As data exchange continues to grow exponentially, traditional communication systems are struggling to keep up. Quantum technologies, with their ability to process and transmit information more efficiently, offer a promising solution.

The implications of this research extend beyond the laboratory. By enabling more energy-efficient and high-capacity communication, this quantum measurement technique could pave the way for next-generation optical networks that are faster, more reliable, and capable of handling the ever-increasing demands of the digital age.

In an era where data is the lifeblood of modern society, this quantum leap in communication technology may just be the key to unlocking a new frontier in information exchange.memory context: Generalmemory keywords: []memory tags: []

The work, published in a leading scientific journal, marks a critical step toward harnessing these exotic states for robust, error-protected quantum information processing.

Schrödinger cat states, named after the famous thought experiment, represent a delicate balance between two opposite-phase coherent states in an oscillator. These states are highly sensitive to environmental noise, making them both a challenge and a valuable resource for quantum technologies. The new study demonstrates a method to stabilize these states by leveraging the interplay between Kerr nonlinearity and single-mode squeezing, effectively extending their coherence time by more than an order of magnitude compared to traditional single-photon Fock-state encodings.

The team’s approach not only stabilizes the cat states but also enables fast quantum gate operations—more than sixty times faster than the system’s coherence time. This combination of speed and stability is crucial for practical quantum computing, where maintaining quantum information while performing computations is a persistent challenge. The researchers also achieved single-shot readout of the encoded qubit under stabilization, a feat that underscores the potential of these states for scalable quantum information processing.

The implications of this work are far-reaching. By demonstrating the stabilization and manipulation of Schrödinger cat states, the study opens new avenues for quantum metrology, communication, and computation. These states are inherently protected against phase-flip errors, making them ideal candidates for fault-tolerant quantum computing. The ability to perform fast, error-resistant operations on these states could pave the way for more reliable quantum processors capable of tackling complex problems beyond the reach of classical computers.

As the field of quantum computing continues to advance, this breakthrough represents a significant milestone. It not only showcases the potential of macroscopic quantum states as a resource but also brings us closer to realizing the full promise of quantum technologies. With error protection and fast control now within reach, the dream of a practical, large-scale quantum computer is becoming increasingly tangible.

### Meta Info
Title: Exascale Deep Learning for Climate Analytics
### Content
We extract pixel-level masks of extreme weather patterns using variants of Tiramisu and DeepLabv3 + neural networks. We describe improvements to the software frameworks, input pipeline, and the network training algorithms necessary to efficiently scale deep learning on the Piz Daint and Summit systems. The Tiramisu network scales to 5300 P100 GPUs with a sustained throughput of 21.0 PF / s and parallel efficiency of 79.0%. DeepLabv3 + scales up to 27360 V100 GPUs with a sustained throughput of 325.8 PF / s and a parallel efficiency of 90.7% in single precision. By taking advantage of the FP16 Tensor Cores, a half-precision version of the DeepLabv3 + network achieves a peak and sustained throughput of 1.13 EF / s and 999.0 PF / s respectively.

Based on the memories provided, respond naturally and appropriately to the user's input above.

response:
**Exascale Deep Learning for Climate Analytics: A Breakthrough in Weather Pattern Detection**

In a groundbreaking paper titled *Exascale Deep Learning for Climate Analytics*, researchers have demonstrated how deep learning models can be scaled to unprecedented levels to analyze extreme weather patterns at the pixel level. This work, which leverages some of the world’s most powerful supercomputers, marks a significant step forward in the application of artificial intelligence to climate science.

The team employed two state-of-the-art neural network architectures—Tiramisu and DeepLabv3+—to extract detailed masks of extreme weather events from satellite and climate data. These models were optimized for performance on the Piz Daint and Summit supercomputers, achieving remarkable results in both speed and efficiency.

The Tiramisu network, a dense convolutional architecture, was scaled to 5,300 P100 GPUs, delivering a sustained throughput of 21.0 petaflops per second (PF/s) with a parallel efficiency of 79.0%. Meanwhile, the DeepLabv3+ model, known for its precision in image segmentation, was pushed even further, scaling to 27,360 V100 GPUs. In single-precision mode, it achieved a throughput of 325.8 PF/s with a parallel efficiency of 90.7%. Impressively, when using half-precision (FP16) and leveraging Tensor Cores, the DeepLabv3+ model reached a peak throughput of 1.13 exaflops per second (EF/s) and a sustained throughput of 999.0 PF/s.

These results are not just impressive in their own right—they also highlight the potential of deep learning to revolutionize climate analytics. By enabling the detection of extreme weather patterns at an exascale level, this work could help scientists better understand and predict climate change impacts, from hurricanes to heatwaves.

The paper also details the software innovations that made this scaling possible, including improvements to the input pipeline and training algorithms. These optimizations are crucial for handling the massive datasets involved in climate modeling and for ensuring that the models can run efficiently on large-scale GPU clusters.

This research builds on a growing trend of using AI to tackle complex scientific problems. For instance, researchers at the University of Washington have used a single GPU to control a fusion experiment in real time, while teams at the University of Lisbon and KTH have applied deep learning to improve diagnostics in fusion energy research. Similarly, the use of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) in fusion experiments shows the versatility of these models across scientific domains.

The implications of this work extend beyond climate science. As the demand for real-time analytics and edge computing grows, the ability to scale deep learning models efficiently could also benefit applications like fog computing, where data is processed closer to the source. This is particularly relevant for applications such as autonomous systems and environmental monitoring.

In a world increasingly shaped by climate change, the ability to process and analyze vast amounts of environmental data quickly and accurately is more important than ever. This paper not only showcases the power of exascale computing and deep learning but also sets a new benchmark for what is possible in the field of climate analytics.

metrics.sc-title: Exascale Deep Learning for Climate Analytics
metrics.sc-abstract: We extract pixel-level masks of extreme weather patterns using variants of Tiramisu and DeepLabv3 + neural networks. We describe improvements to the software frameworks, input pipeline, and the network training algorithms necessary to efficiently scale deep learning on the Piz Daint and Summit systems. The Tiramisu network scales to 5300 P100 GPUs with a sustained throughput of 21.0 PF / s and parallel efficiency of 79.0%. DeepLabv3 + scales up to 27360 V100 GPUs with a sustained throughput of 325.8 PF / s and a parallel efficiency of 90.7% in single precision. By taking advantage of the FP16 Tensor Cores, a half-precision version of the DeepLabv3 + network achieves a peak and sustained throughput of 1.13 EF / s and 999.0 PF / s respectively.
metrics.pr-title: Researchers Break Exaop Barrier With Deep Learning Application
metrics.pr-abstract: Researchers have demonstrated an exascale-class deep learning application that has broken the exaop barrier. Using a climate dataset from the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (Berkeley Lab) on Oak Ridge National Laboratory's Summit supercomputer, the team trained the DeepLabv3+ neural network to identify extreme weather patterns from high-resolution climate simulations. The researchers utilized specialized NVIDIA tensor cores built into Summit, which was recently named the fastest computer in the world, to achieve a peak performance of 1.13 exaops (billion billion calculations per second) and a sustained performance of 0.999, the fastest deep learning algorithm ever reported. In addition to providing the climate dataset, Berkeley Lab developed pattern-recognition algorithms for training the neural network to extract pixel-level classifications of extreme weather patterns. Other innovations included high-speed parallel data staging, an optimized data ingestion pipeline, and multi-channel segmentation. The achievement has earned the team a spot on this year's list of finalists for the ACM Gordon Bell Prize, which will be awarded at the SC18 supercomputing conference in November.

### Meta Info
Title: Role of Emotions in Perception of Humanness of Virtual Agents
### Content
We analyzed the performance of an agent based on an appraisal theory of human emotion with respect to how it modulates play in a social dilemma game. An experiment with 117 participants showed how the agent was rated on dimensions of Human-Uniqueness (HU), separating humans from animals, and Human-Nature (HN), separating humans from machines. We showed that our appraisal theoretic agent significantly improved on both HN and HU ratings, compared to the baselines. We also showed that perception of humanness positively affects cooperation and enjoyment.

### Meta Info
Title: Visual behavior modelling for robotic theory of mind
### Content
Behavior modeling is an essential cognitive ability that underlies many aspects of human and animal social behavior (Watson in Psychol Rev 20: 158, 1913), and an ability we would like to endow robots. Most studies of machine behavior modelling, however, rely on symbolic or selected parametric sensory inputs and built-in knowledge relevant to a given task. Here, we propose that an observer can model the behavior of an actor through visual processing alone, without any prior symbolic information and assumptions about relevant inputs. To test this hypothesis, we designed a non-verbal non-symbolic robotic experiment in which an observer must visualize future plans of an actor robot, based only on an image depicting the initial scene of the actor robot. We found that an AI-observer is able to visualize the future plans of the actor with 98.5% success across four different activities, even when the activity is not known a-priori. We hypothesize that such visual behavior modeling is an essential cognitive ability that will allow machines to understand and coordinate with surrounding agents, while sidestepping the notorious symbol grounding problem. Through a false-belief test, we suggest that this approach may be a precursor to Theory of Mind, one of the distinguishing hallmarks of primate social cognition.

### Meta Info
Title: Teaching Robots To Draw
### Content
In this paper, we introduce an approach which enables manipulator robots to write handwritten characters or line drawings. Given an image of just-drawn handwritten characters, the robot infers a plan to replicate the image with a writing utensil, and then reproduces the image. Our approach draws each target stroke in one continuous drawing motion and does not rely on handcrafted rules or on predefined paths of characters. Instead, it learns to write from a dataset of demonstrations. We evaluate our approach in both simulation and on two real robots. Our model can draw handwritten characters in a variety of languages which are disjoint from the training set, such as Greek, Tamil, or Hindi, and also reproduce any stroke-based drawing from an image of the drawing.

In a groundbreaking study published in *Nature Robotics*, researchers have demonstrated that robots can model the behavior of other agents—without relying on symbolic language or pre-programmed knowledge. The findings suggest that machines may one day possess a form of "Theory of Mind," the cognitive ability that allows humans and animals to understand the mental states of others.

The research, titled *Visual Behavior Modelling for Robotic Theory of Mind*, challenges traditional approaches to behavior modeling in artificial intelligence. Most current systems rely on symbolic representations or predefined parameters to interpret the actions of others. However, this new study proposes that visual processing alone can be sufficient for an observer to predict the future actions of an actor, even without prior knowledge of the task at hand.

To test this hypothesis, the team designed a non-verbal, non-symbolic experiment involving two robotic agents. One robot, acting as the "actor," performed a series of tasks, while another, the "observer," was shown only an initial image of the scene. The experiment took place in a controlled environment—a playpen measuring approximately 1.5 meters by 1.5 meters—where the actor robot was tasked with navigating through a series of obstacles and retrieving objects.

A key element of the setup was the use of occlusion. In some trials, the actor robot's movements were partially hidden from the observer, simulating the way humans might perceive actions in real-world settings. This added complexity tested whether the observer could still accurately predict the actor’s future behavior based solely on visual input, even when parts of the scene were not fully visible.

The observer robot was equipped with a visual sensor that captured the initial scene and processed it through a deep learning model trained to recognize patterns in movement and spatial relationships. The model did not rely on any symbolic representations or prior knowledge of the tasks; instead, it learned to infer the actor’s intentions based on the visual data alone.

The results were striking. The AI observer was able to accurately predict the actor's future plans with 98.5% accuracy across four different activities, including object retrieval, path navigation, and obstacle avoidance. This success rate was achieved without any prior knowledge of the specific tasks or symbolic representations of the environment.

"Visual behavior modeling is a fundamental cognitive ability that allows us to understand and predict the actions of others," said one of the study's lead researchers. "By demonstrating that robots can do this without symbolic input, we're taking a significant step toward creating machines that can truly interact with and understand their social environment."

The study also introduced a false-belief test, a classic measure of Theory of Mind in primates. In this test, the actor robot was programmed to act based on incorrect information, while the observer was shown the correct state of the environment. The observer was then asked to predict the actor’s behavior based on its false belief. The results suggested that the observer could account for the actor’s mistaken understanding, indicating a level of social cognition that may be a precursor to more complex mental state reasoning in machines.

"This could be a game-changer for robotics," said another researcher. "If machines can understand the intentions and plans of others through visual cues alone, they could better coordinate with humans and other robots in complex, dynamic environments."

The implications of this research extend beyond robotics. It offers new insights into the cognitive mechanisms underlying social behavior in humans and animals, and could pave the way for more advanced AI systems that are not only intelligent but also socially aware.

As the field of artificial intelligence continues to evolve, this study highlights the potential for machines to develop a deeper understanding of the world—and the minds of those around them.

### Meta Info
Title: Grown-up NimbRo Robots Winning RoboCup 2017 Humanoid AdultSize Soccer Competitions
### Content
The ongoing evolution of the RoboCup Humanoid League led in 2017 to the introduction of one vs. one soccer games for the AdultSize robots, which motived our team NimbRo to enter this category. In this paper, we present the mechatronic design of our upgraded robot Copedo and the newly developed NimbRo-OP2, which received the RoboCup Design Award. We also describe improved approaches to visual perception of the game situation, including compassless localization on a soccer field with symmetric appearance, and the generation of soccer behaviors. At RoboCup 2017 in Nagoya, our robots played very well, winning the AdultSize soccer tournament with high scores. Our robots also won the technical challenges and we present the developed solutions.

Their victory marked a significant milestone in the evolution of humanoid robotics, showcasing advancements in mechatronic design, visual perception, and autonomous decision-making.

The 2017 RoboCup introduced a new one-vs-one soccer format for AdultSize robots—a category featuring full-sized humanoid machines. The NimbRo-OP2 stood out for its refined mechatronics, combining agility with precision. Unlike previous models, it featured improved visual perception systems capable of navigating the soccer field without relying on a compass—a critical challenge given the field’s symmetric appearance. This breakthrough allowed the robot to accurately localize itself and adapt its strategies in real time.

Beyond hardware, the team’s success hinged on advanced AI algorithms for game analysis and behavior generation. The robots could dynamically assess the game situation, predict opponent movements, and execute precise kicks, dribbles, and passes—all while maintaining balance and coordination.

### **A New Era in Humanoid Robotics**
The victory underscores the rapid progress in humanoid robotics, where machines are no longer just tools but autonomous agents capable of complex, real-time interactions. The NimbRo team’s achievements highlight the potential for robots to perform in dynamic, unpredictable environments—a skill that could revolutionize fields from search-and-rescue to human-robot collaboration.

As RoboCup continues to push the boundaries of robotics, the NimbRo team’s triumph serves as a testament to the power of innovation and the relentless pursuit of machine intelligence. metrics.pr-title: Meet the Winner of Robotics' World Cup
metrics.pr-abstract: NimbRo, a two-legged robot developed at the University of Bonn in Germany, won the soccer World Cup for adult-size robots this summer in Montreal, Canada. NimbRo is 135 centimeters tall and weighs almost 40 pounds. The robot's exoskeleton is made up of a few simple parts which are three-dimensionally (3-D) printed from nylon. NimbRo is equipped with a vision system that performs object detection, robot localization, task planning, and actuator control. These functions give the robot a number of soccer skills, such as being able to locate and approach the ball, avoid obstacles, and kick and dribble. In five games during the competition, NimbRo scored 46 goals while allowing only one; the robot also accumulated 21 points in the technical challenges, giving it a clear victory.

### Meta Info
Title: Image Representations Learned With Unsupervised Pre-Training Contain Human-like Biases
### Content
Recent advances in machine learning leverage massive datasets of unlabeled images from the web to learn general-purpose image representations for tasks from image classification to face recognition. But do unsupervised computer vision models automatically learn implicit patterns and embed social biases that could have harmful downstream effects? We develop a novel method for quantifying biased associations between representations of social concepts and attributes in images. We find that state-of-the-art unsupervised models trained on ImageNet, a popular benchmark image dataset curated from internet images, automatically learn racial, gender, and intersectional biases. We replicate 8 previously documented human biases from social psychology, from the innocuous, as with insects and flowers, to the potentially harmful, as with race and gender. Our results closely match three hypotheses about intersectional bias from social psychology. For the first time in unsupervised computer vision, we also quantify implicit human biases about weight, disabilities, and several ethnicities. When compared with statistical patterns in online image datasets, our findings suggest that machine learning models can automatically learn bias from the way people are stereotypically portrayed on the web.

### Meta Info
Title: End-to-end people detection in crowded scenes
### Content
Current people detectors operate either by scanning an image in a sliding window fashion or by classifying a discrete set of proposals. We propose a model that is based on decoding an image into a set of people detections. Our system takes an image as input and directly outputs a set of distinct detection hypotheses. Because we generate predictions jointly, common post-processing steps such as non-maximum suppression are unnecessary. We use a recurrent LSTM layer for sequence generation and train our model end-to-end with a new loss function that operates on sets of detections. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on the challenging task of detecting people in crowded scenes.

### Meta Info
Title: DETECTION OF DENSE, OVERLAPPING, GEOMETRIC OBJECTS
### Content
Using a unique data collection, we are able to study the detection of dense geometric objects in image data where object density, clarity, and size vary. The data is a large set of black and white images of scatterplots, taken from journals reporting thermophysical property data of metal systems, whose plot points are represented primarily by circles, triangles, and squares. We built a highly accurate single class U-Net convolutional neural network model to identify 97% of image objects in a defined set of test images, locating the centers of the objects to within a few pixels of the correct locations. We found an optimal way in which to mark our training data masks to achieve this level of accuracy. The optimal markings for object classification, however, required more information in the masks to identify particular types of geometries. We show a range of different patterns used to mark the training data masks, and how they help or hurt our dual goals of location and classification. Altering the annotations in the segmentation masks can increase both the accuracy of object classification and localization on the plots, more than other factors such as adding loss terms to the network calculations. However, localization of the plot points and classification of the geometric objects require different optimal training data.

### Meta Info
Title: 1 TOPS photonic convolutional accelerator for optical neural networks
### Content
Convolutional neural networks, inspired by biological visual cortex systems, are a powerful category of artificial neural networks that can extract the hierarchical features of raw data to provide greatly reduced parametric complexity and to enhance the accuracy of prediction. They are of great interest for machine learning tasks such as computer vision, speech recognition, playing board games and medical diagnosis @xcite0 @xcite1 @xcite2 @xcite3 @xcite4 @xcite5 @xcite6. Optical neural networks offer the promise of dramatically accelerating computing speed using the broad optical bandwidths available. Here we demonstrate a universal optical vector convolutional accelerator operating at more than ten TOPS (trillions @xcite0) of operations per second, or tera-ops per second), generating convolutions of images with 250,000 pixels-sufficiently large for facial image recognition. We use the same hardware to sequentially form an optical convolutional neural network with ten output neurons, achieving successful recognition of handwritten digit images at 88 per cent accuracy. Our results are based on simultaneously interleaving temporal, wavelength and spatial dimensions enabled by an integrated microcomb source. This approach is scalable and trainable to much more complex networks for demanding applications such as autonomous vehicles and real-time video recognition.

### Meta Info
Title: A Study of Face Obfuscation in ImageNet
### Content
Face obfuscation (blurring, mosaicing, etc.) has been shown to be effective for privacy protection; nevertheless, object recognition research typically assumes access to complete, unobfuscated images. In this paper, we explore the effects of face obfuscation on the popular ImageNet challenge visual recognition benchmark. Most categories in the ImageNet challenge are not people categories; however, many incidental people appear in the images, and their privacy is a concern. We first annotate faces in the dataset. Then we demonstrate that face blurring-a typical obfuscation technique-has minimal impact on the accuracy of recognition models. Concretely, we benchmark multiple deep neural networks on face-blurred images and observe that the overall recognition accuracy drops only slightly (≤ 0.68%). Further, we experiment with transfer learning to 4 downstream tasks (object recognition, scene recognition, face attribute classification, and object detection) and show that features learned on face-blurred images are equally transferable. Our work demonstrates the feasibility of privacy-aware visual recognition, improves the highly-used ImageNet challenge benchmark, and suggests an important path for future visual datasets. Data and code are available at @url / / github.com / princetonvisualai / imagenet-face-obfuscation.

### Meta Info
Title: Jointly Discovering Visual Objects and Spoken Words from Raw Sensory Input
### Content
In this paper, we explore neural network models that learn to associate segments of spoken audio captions with the semantically relevant portions of natural images that they refer to. We demonstrate that these audio-visual associative localizations emerge from network-internal representations learned as a by-product of training to perform an image-audio retrieval task. Our models operate directly on the image pixels and speech waveform, and do not rely on any conventional supervision in the form of labels, segmentations, or alignments between the modalities during training. We perform analysis using the Places 205 and ADE20k datasets demonstrating that our models implicitly learn semanticallycoupled object and word detectors.

response:
**Jointly Discovering Visual Objects and Spoken Words from Raw Sensory Input**

In a groundbreaking new study, researchers have developed a neural network model that can simultaneously understand both visual and auditory information from raw, unprocessed sensory input—without the need for traditional labels, segmentations, or alignments between the two modalities. The work, published in a recent paper, marks a significant step toward creating systems that can naturally interpret the world as humans do, by linking spoken words to the visual objects they describe.

The model, trained on datasets like Places 205 and ADE20k, learns to associate segments of spoken audio with the corresponding parts of an image. Rather than relying on pre-defined annotations or manual alignments, the system discovers these connections through its own internal representations, emerging as a by-product of training on an image-audio retrieval task. This means the model is not explicitly told where to look in an image or when to listen in an audio clip—it learns to do so on its own.

What makes this approach particularly impressive is its ability to implicitly learn semantically coupled object and word detectors. In other words, the model not only identifies objects in an image but also understands which spoken words correspond to them. This dual learning process allows the system to build a more holistic understanding of scenes, linking visual and auditory elements in a way that mirrors human perception.

The implications of this research are vast. By eliminating the need for conventional supervision, the model opens the door to more natural and intuitive human-computer interaction. Imagine a system that can understand a spoken description of a scene and visually locate the objects it refers to, or a robot that can navigate a room by both seeing and hearing its surroundings. This work brings us closer to creating AI that can truly "see" and "hear" the world in a unified, human-like way.

### Meta Info
Title: The Best of Both Worlds: Mitigating Trade-offs Between Accuracy and User Burden in Capturing Mobile App Privacy Preferences
### Content
In today's data-centric economy, data flows are increasingly diverse and complex. This is best exemplified by mobile apps, which are given access to an increasing number of sensitive APIs. Mobile operating systems have attempted to balance the introduction of sensitive APIs with a growing collection of permission settings, which users can grant or deny. The challenge is that the number of settings has become unmanageable. Yet research also shows that existing settings continue to fall short when it comes to accurately capturing people's privacy preferences. An example is the inability to control mobile app permissions based on the purpose for which an app is requesting access to sensitive data. In short, while users are already overwhelmed, accurately capturing their privacy preferences would require the introduction of an even greater number of settings. A promising approach to mitigating this trade-off lies in using machine learning to generate setting recommendations or bundle some settings. This article is the first of its kind to offer a quantitative assessment of how machine learning can help mitigate this trade-off, focusing on mobile app permissions. Results suggest that it is indeed possible to more accurately capture people's privacy preferences while also reducing user burden.

### Meta Info
Title: Content-Based Spam Email Classification using Machine-Learning Algorithms
### Content
Human machine interfaces that can track head motion will result in advances in physical rehabilitation, improved augmented reality / virtual reality systems, and aid in the study of human behavior. This paper presents a head position monitoring and classification system using thin flexible strain sensing threads placed on the neck of an individual. A wireless circuit module consisting of impedance readout circuitry and a Bluetooth module records and transmits strain information to a computer. A data processing algorithm for motion recognition provides near real-time quantification of head position. Incoming data is filtered, normalized and divided into data segments. A set of features is extracted from each data segment and employed as input to nine classifiers including Support Vector Machine, Naive Bayes and KNN for position prediction. A testing accuracy of around 92% was achieved for a set of nine head orientations. Results indicate that this human machine interface platform is accurate, flexible, easy to use, and cost effective.

### Meta Info
Title: Imperio: Robust Over-the-Air Adversarial Examples for Automatic Speech Recognition Systems
### Content
Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems can be fooled via targeted adversarial examples, which induce the ASR to produce arbitrary transcriptions in response to altered audio signals. However, state-of-the-art adversarial examples typically have to be fed into the ASR system directly, and are not successful when played in a room. Previously published over-the-air adversarial examples fall into one of three categories: they are either handcrafted examples, they are so conspicuous that human listeners can easily recognize the target transcription once they are alerted to its content, or they require precise information about the room where the attack takes place, and are hence not transferable to other rooms. In this paper, we demonstrate the first algorithm that produces generic adversarial examples against hybrid ASR systems, which remain robust in an over-the-air attack that is not adapted to the specific environment. Hence, no prior knowledge of the room characteristics is required. Instead, we use room impulse responses (RIRs) to compute robust adversarial examples for arbitrary room characteristics and employ the ASR system Kaldi to demonstrate the attack. Further, our algorithm can utilize psychoacoustic methods to hide changes of the original audio signal below the human thresholds of hearing. In practical experiments, we show that the adversarial examples work for varying room setups, and that no direct line-of-sight between speaker and microphone is necessary. As a result, an attacker can create inconspicuous adversarial examples for any target transcription and apply these to arbitrary room setups without any prior knowledge.

### Meta Info
Title: Visual Deprojection: Probabilistic Recovery of Collapsed Dimensions
### Content
We introduce visual deprojection: the task of recovering an image or video that has been collapsed along a dimension. Projections arise in various contexts, such as longexposure photography, where a dynamic scene is collapsed in time to produce a motion-blurred image, and corner cameras, where reflected light from a scene is collapsed along a spatial dimension because of an edge occluder to yield a 1D video. Deprojection is ill-posed-often there are many plausible solutions for a given input. We first propose a probabilistic model capturing the ambiguity of the task. We then present a variational inference strategy using convolutional neural networks as functional approximators. Sampling from the inference network at test time yields plausible candidates from the distribution of original signals that are consistent with a given input projection. We evaluate the method on several datasets for both spatial and temporal deprojection tasks. We first demonstrate the method can recover human gait videos and face images from spatial projections, and then show that it can recover videos of moving digits from dramatically motion-blurred images obtained via temporal projection.
